questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral lower extremity edema. He has no significant past medical history and denies alcohol use. Echocardiography reveals four-chamber dilation with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 18% and global hypokinesis. Endomyocardial biopsy shows myocyte hypertrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and no inflammatory infiltrate. Genetic testing reveals a truncating variant in the TTN gene. Which of the following best describes the primary molecular mechanism leading to this patient's cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Defective calcium cycling due to mutations in the ryanodine receptor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of sarcomeric structural integrity due to defective titin protein

choices.label: C
choices.text: Impaired desmosomal cell-cell adhesion between cardiomyocytes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Toxic accumulation of misfolded immunoglobulin light chains in the interstitium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autoimmune-mediated destruction of beta-1 adrenergic receptors

explanation.summary: TTN truncating variants are the most common genetic cause of dilated cardiomyopathy, accounting for approximately 20-25% of familial DCM cases. Titin is the largest known protein and serves as a molecular spring within the sarcomere, spanning from the Z-disc to the M-line.
explanation.whyCorrect: Titin provides passive elasticity to the sarcomere and serves as a scaffold for sarcomere assembly and mechanosensing. Truncating mutations produce haploinsufficiency of functional titin, leading to loss of sarcomeric structural integrity, impaired force generation, and progressive ventricular dilation with systolic dysfunction. The resulting cardiomyopathy is characterized by eccentric remodeling and global hypokinesis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ryanodine receptor (RYR2) mutations are associated with catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT) and some forms of arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy, not typical dilated cardiomyopathy with global hypokinesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Defective desmosomal adhesion (mutations in DSP, PKP2, DSG2, DSC2, JUP) is the hallmark of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC), which predominantly affects the right ventricle with fibrofatty replacement, not four-chamber dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Accumulation of misfolded light chains describes AL amyloidosis, which causes restrictive cardiomyopathy with thickened ventricular walls and diastolic dysfunction, not the dilated phenotype with thin walls seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autoimmune anti-beta-1 receptor antibodies have been described in some DCM cases but are not the mechanism in genetically confirmed TTN-related cardiomyopathy. This patient has an identified genetic etiology.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with fatigue and exertional dyspnea 4 weeks after a viral upper respiratory infection. Physical examination reveals an S3 gallop and bibasilar crackles. Echocardiography shows a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% with diffuse hypokinesis. Cardiac MRI demonstrates diffuse mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement. Endomyocardial biopsy reveals lymphocytic infiltration with adjacent myocyte necrosis. Which of the following is the most likely pathogenesis of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery vasospasm triggered by circulating viral antigens

choices.label: B
choices.text: Granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cell formation in the myocardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eosinophilic infiltration with release of major basic protein causing myocyte toxicity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct cytotoxic injury from viral replication within cardiomyocytes followed by autoimmune-mediated damage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Microvascular thrombosis from complement-mediated endothelial injury

explanation.summary: This presentation is classic for viral myocarditis progressing to dilated cardiomyopathy. The post-viral timeline, lymphocytic infiltrate with myocyte necrosis on biopsy, and diffuse mid-wall LGE on MRI all support this diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Viral myocarditis progresses through three phases: (1) direct viral cytotoxicity from viral entry and replication in cardiomyocytes (e.g., coxsackievirus B via the CAR receptor), (2) innate and adaptive immune activation with lymphocytic infiltration, and (3) potential persistent autoimmune injury through molecular mimicry between viral and cardiac antigens (such as cardiac myosin). This can lead to chronic inflammation, fibrosis, and a DCM phenotype.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary vasospasm (Prinzmetal angina) causes focal ischemic injury in a coronary territory, not diffuse hypokinesis. Viral myocarditis affects the myocardium directly, not through coronary vasospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Granulomatous inflammation with giant cells describes giant cell myocarditis, which has a much more fulminant course and distinct histology with giant cells. This biopsy specifically shows lymphocytic infiltration without granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Eosinophilic myocarditis is caused by eosinophilic infiltration (seen in hypereosinophilic syndrome, drug hypersensitivity) and would show eosinophils rather than lymphocytes on biopsy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Complement-mediated microvascular thrombosis describes thrombotic microangiopathy (e.g., HUS/TTP), not the pathogenesis of viral myocarditis. The biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltration, not microvascular thrombi.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a 20-year history of heavy alcohol use (approximately 10 drinks per day) presents with progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, and lower extremity edema. Physical examination reveals jugular venous distension, hepatomegaly, and a displaced point of maximal impulse. Echocardiography shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 22%. Which of the following mechanisms most directly contributes to the development of this patient's cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thiamine deficiency causing impaired oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Iron deposition within cardiomyocytes leading to oxidative damage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct toxic effects of acetaldehyde on mitochondrial function and calcium homeostasis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cobalt contamination of alcoholic beverages causing myofibrillar destruction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autoantibody formation against cardiac myosin heavy chain

explanation.summary: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is a form of dilated cardiomyopathy caused by chronic heavy alcohol use, typically after more than 10 years of consumption exceeding 80 g/day. The primary mechanism involves direct toxic effects of ethanol and its metabolite acetaldehyde on the myocardium.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acetaldehyde, the primary metabolite of ethanol via alcohol dehydrogenase, directly damages cardiomyocytes through multiple mechanisms: mitochondrial dysfunction with impaired oxidative phosphorylation, disruption of intracellular calcium handling, inhibition of protein synthesis, increased oxidative stress through reactive oxygen species generation, and promotion of apoptosis. These effects lead to progressive myocyte loss and replacement fibrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While thiamine deficiency (wet beriberi) can cause high-output heart failure in alcoholics, it produces a distinct clinical syndrome and is not the primary mechanism of alcoholic dilated cardiomyopathy. Wet beriberi is treated with thiamine replacement; DCM from alcohol toxicity is not.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron deposition describes hemochromatosis cardiomyopathy, which can present similarly but is caused by hereditary or transfusion-related iron overload, not alcohol use. Serum iron studies and MRI T2* mapping would differentiate this condition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cobalt-induced cardiomyopathy was a historical entity ('beer drinker's cardiomyopathy') from cobalt used as a foam stabilizer in beer in the 1960s. This is not a current mechanism and is distinct from standard alcoholic cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autoantibody formation may contribute to the pathogenesis of idiopathic DCM but is not the primary mechanism in alcoholic cardiomyopathy, where direct toxic injury from acetaldehyde predominates.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 36 weeks pregnant presents to the emergency department with acute onset of dyspnea, orthopnea, and pink frothy sputum. Her blood pressure is 100/65 mmHg and pulse is 112/min. Echocardiography reveals a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20% with severe biventricular dilation. She has no prior cardiac history. Brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) is 1800 pg/mL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preeclampsia-induced hypertensive heart failure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amniotic fluid embolism with acute cor pulmonale

choices.label: C
choices.text: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy from emotional stress of pregnancy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peripartum cardiomyopathy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulmonary embolism with right heart failure

explanation.summary: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is a form of dilated cardiomyopathy presenting with systolic dysfunction (EF <45%) in the last month of pregnancy through 5 months postpartum, in the absence of another identifiable cause of heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient meets all diagnostic criteria for PPCM: presentation in the last month of pregnancy, severe systolic dysfunction (EF 20%), no prior cardiac history, and no other identifiable cause. The pathogenesis involves a cleaved 16-kDa prolactin fragment with antiangiogenic and proapoptotic properties, oxidative stress, and abnormal immune activation. The severe biventricular dilation with markedly elevated BNP reflects the dilated cardiomyopathy phenotype.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Preeclampsia-induced heart failure would present with hypertension (BP >140/90), proteinuria, and typically diastolic dysfunction from pressure overload. This patient is hypotensive, not hypertensive, making preeclampsia unlikely.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amniotic fluid embolism presents with sudden cardiovascular collapse, DIC, and respiratory failure, typically during labor or immediately postpartum. It does not cause chronic biventricular dilation, and this patient is not in active labor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy causes characteristic apical ballooning with basal hyperkinesis, not severe global biventricular dilation. It is triggered by acute emotional or physical stress and has a distinct wall motion pattern on echocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Massive pulmonary embolism causes acute right heart failure and cor pulmonale with right ventricular dilation, not biventricular dilation with severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction. It would also present with acute hypoxemia and elevated D-dimer.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer treated with doxorubicin (cumulative dose 550 mg/m²) 8 months ago presents with progressive exercise intolerance. Echocardiography reveals an ejection fraction of 32%, down from 60% prior to chemotherapy. Endomyocardial biopsy shows vacuolar degeneration of myocytes with myofibrillar loss. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of myocardial injury in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of vascular endothelial growth factor receptor signaling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Formation of reactive oxygen species through iron-dependent redox cycling of the drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immune checkpoint activation causing T-cell-mediated myocarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery vasospasm leading to demand ischemia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct inhibition of the cardiac sodium channel causing conduction abnormalities

explanation.summary: Doxorubicin (anthracycline) cardiotoxicity is dose-dependent and cumulative, with risk increasing significantly above 400 mg/m². The classic histologic finding is vacuolar degeneration with myofibrillar loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: Doxorubicin forms complexes with iron (Fe³⁺), generating free radicals through redox cycling. The resulting reactive oxygen species cause lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial damage, DNA double-strand breaks, and activation of apoptotic pathways in cardiomyocytes. Cardiomyocytes are particularly vulnerable because they have low levels of catalase and rely on the topoisomerase IIβ isoform, which doxorubicin poisons, causing mitochondrial dysfunction and cell death.

whyWrongByChoice.A: VEGF receptor inhibition describes the mechanism of cardiotoxicity from tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as sunitinib and sorafenib, not anthracyclines. These drugs cause hypertension and cardiac dysfunction through impaired angiogenesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated myocarditis occurs with drugs like nivolumab and ipilimumab, not anthracyclines. It presents with lymphocytic myocarditis, not the vacuolar degeneration seen on this patient's biopsy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary vasospasm is associated with 5-fluorouracil and capecitabine cardiotoxicity, not anthracyclines. Doxorubicin causes direct myocyte injury independent of coronary blood flow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sodium channel inhibition describes the mechanism of certain antiarrhythmic drugs (class I agents) and causes conduction abnormalities, not the progressive dilated cardiomyopathy seen with cumulative anthracycline exposure.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 15% is being managed with guideline-directed medical therapy including sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone, and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Despite optimal therapy, he remains NYHA class III with recurrent hospitalizations. His QRS duration on ECG is 160 ms with a left bundle branch block morphology. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addition of digoxin to improve contractility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Referral for cardiac catheterization with percutaneous coronary intervention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiation of continuous intravenous milrinone infusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implantation of a cardiac resynchronization therapy defibrillator (CRT-D)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Surgical myectomy of the interventricular septum

explanation.summary: This patient meets all criteria for cardiac resynchronization therapy: NYHA class II-IV symptoms despite optimal medical therapy, LVEF ≤35%, and QRS ≥150 ms with LBBB morphology. CRT-D is strongly indicated (Class I recommendation).
explanation.whyCorrect: CRT-D provides biventricular pacing that restores synchronous contraction, improving ejection fraction, reducing mitral regurgitation, and promoting reverse remodeling. The strongest benefit is seen in patients with LBBB and QRS ≥150 ms, where dyssynchrony is most pronounced. The defibrillator component provides protection against sudden cardiac death, which is the most common cause of death in DCM patients with severe systolic dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While digoxin can reduce HF hospitalizations, it does not improve mortality and would be a less impactful intervention than CRT-D in a patient with clear indication for device therapy. Digoxin does not address the electrical dyssynchrony causing his refractory symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no indication of coronary artery disease in this patient. He has a non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy, and catheterization with PCI would not address the underlying cardiomyopathy or the LBBB dyssynchrony.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Continuous intravenous milrinone is reserved for end-stage patients as a bridge to transplant or destination therapy. It increases mortality when used chronically and is not appropriate before CRT has been attempted.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surgical myectomy is the treatment for hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) with symptomatic LVOT obstruction. It has no role in dilated cardiomyopathy, where the septum is thin, not hypertrophied.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 25% is found to have a large mural thrombus in the left ventricular apex on echocardiography. He is in normal sinus rhythm. Which of the following best explains why patients with dilated cardiomyopathy are at increased risk for intracardiac thrombus formation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Turbulent flow across the left ventricular outflow tract creates high shear stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood stasis in a dilated, poorly contracting ventricle fulfills Virchow's triad

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation is universally present in dilated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic anticoagulant deficiency due to hepatic congestion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endocardial fibrosis creates a prothrombotic surface exclusively in the right ventricle

explanation.summary: Intracardiac thrombus formation in DCM is primarily due to blood stasis in a dilated, poorly contracting ventricle, fulfilling the stasis component of Virchow's triad. The LV apex is the most common site due to its relative stagnation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DCM, the dilated and hypokinetic left ventricle creates regions of stagnant blood flow, particularly at the apex. This fulfills Virchow's triad: stasis (poor contraction and dilation), endothelial injury (from chronic distension and wall stress), and hypercoagulability (from neurohormonal activation and inflammatory cytokines). The combination markedly increases thrombus risk and potential for systemic embolization, including stroke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Turbulent flow across the LVOT is characteristic of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, not DCM. In DCM, flow velocities are low due to poor contractility; it is stasis, not turbulence, that promotes thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While atrial fibrillation is common in DCM, it is not universally present. This patient is specifically noted to be in normal sinus rhythm, yet still has a ventricular thrombus, demonstrating that stasis alone in the dilated ventricle is sufficient for thrombus formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While hepatic congestion from right heart failure can impair synthetic function, clinically significant anticoagulant deficiency is not the primary mechanism of intracardiac thrombosis in DCM. The dominant mechanism is blood stasis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endocardial fibrosis with restrictive physiology and right-sided thrombi describes endomyocardial fibrosis (Loeffler endocarditis), a different entity. In DCM, thrombi form predominantly in the left ventricular apex, not exclusively in the right ventricle.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with known dilated cardiomyopathy undergoes cardiac catheterization. Hemodynamic measurements reveal: right atrial pressure 14 mmHg, pulmonary artery pressure 52/28 mmHg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 26 mmHg, cardiac output 3.2 L/min, and systemic vascular resistance 1800 dynes·s/cm⁵. Which of the following compensatory mechanisms is most directly responsible for the elevated systemic vascular resistance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased nitric oxide production from endothelial shear stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Withdrawal of parasympathetic tone to resistance vessels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aortic baroreceptor-mediated reflex vasodilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release of atrial natriuretic peptide causing systemic vasodilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system with angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction

explanation.summary: In advanced DCM with reduced cardiac output, the RAAS is activated as a compensatory mechanism to maintain blood pressure. Angiotensin II is a potent vasoconstrictor that increases SVR, which initially preserves perfusion pressure but ultimately increases afterload and worsens cardiac function.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reduced cardiac output leads to decreased renal perfusion, stimulating renin release from the juxtaglomerular apparatus. Renin converts angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, which ACE converts to angiotensin II. Angiotensin II acts on AT1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle to cause potent vasoconstriction, directly increasing SVR. It also stimulates aldosterone release (causing sodium and water retention) and enhances sympathetic activity. While initially compensatory, chronic RAAS activation is maladaptive and is the target of ACE inhibitors and ARBs in heart failure therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased nitric oxide would cause vasodilation and decrease SVR, the opposite of what is observed. In heart failure, endothelial dysfunction actually reduces NO bioavailability, contributing to impaired vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Parasympathetic (vagal) tone primarily regulates heart rate through the sinoatrial node and has minimal direct effect on peripheral resistance vessels, which lack significant parasympathetic innervation. Sympathetic activation, not parasympathetic withdrawal, drives vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Baroreceptor-mediated vasodilation would lower SVR, but in heart failure the baroreceptor reflex is impaired (baroreflex resetting), with reduced sensitivity. The net effect is enhanced sympathetic outflow, not reflex vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ANP release in response to atrial distension causes vasodilation and natriuresis, which would decrease SVR. While ANP is elevated in heart failure, its vasodilatory effect is overwhelmed by the vasoconstrictive effects of the RAAS and sympathetic nervous system.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman from Central America presents with progressive dyspnea, palpitations, and syncope. ECG shows right bundle branch block with left anterior fascicular block. Echocardiography reveals biventricular dilation with an apical aneurysm and an ejection fraction of 28%. She recalls having a prolonged febrile illness with unilateral eyelid swelling during childhood. Which of the following organisms is most likely responsible for her current cardiac condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coxsackievirus B

choices.label: B
choices.text: Borrelia burgdorferi

choices.label: C
choices.text: Streptococcus pyogenes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Toxoplasma gondii

choices.label: E
choices.text: Trypanosoma cruzi

explanation.summary: This presentation is classic for chronic Chagas cardiomyopathy caused by Trypanosoma cruzi. The key clues include Central American origin, childhood illness with periorbital edema (Romaña sign), RBBB with LAFB, apical aneurysm, and biventricular dilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Trypanosoma cruzi, transmitted by the triatomine (reduviid) bug, causes Chagas disease. The acute phase features the chagoma or Romaña sign (unilateral periorbital edema). After decades of latency, the chronic phase develops in ~30% of infected individuals, causing a distinctive cardiomyopathy characterized by conduction abnormalities (RBBB + LAFB is pathognomonic), apical aneurysms (unique to Chagas among cardiomyopathies), biventricular dilation, and thromboembolic complications.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coxsackievirus B is the most common cause of viral myocarditis in adults but does not cause the characteristic pattern of RBBB with LAFB and apical aneurysm. Viral myocarditis typically presents acutely and lacks the geographic and epidemiologic features of Chagas disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Borrelia burgdorferi (Lyme disease) causes cardiac involvement primarily as AV block (especially high-degree AV block), not RBBB with LAFB and apical aneurysm. Lyme carditis is usually self-limited and does not cause chronic dilated cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Streptococcus pyogenes causes acute rheumatic fever, which affects heart valves (pancarditis with valvulitis), not the myocardium in a pattern of dilated cardiomyopathy with conduction defects and apical aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Toxoplasma gondii can cause myocarditis primarily in immunocompromised patients (e.g., AIDS), but it does not produce the characteristic conduction pattern or apical aneurysm seen in Chagas disease.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 20%) is started on carvedilol. During the first two weeks of therapy, he reports worsening dyspnea and weight gain. His cardiologist advises continuing the medication with dose adjustment of diuretics. Which of the following best explains the long-term benefit of beta-blocker therapy in this patient despite initial clinical worsening?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-blockers increase cardiac output by enhancing calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-blockers reduce heart rate, allowing greater diastolic filling time and coronary perfusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-blockers reverse maladaptive neurohormonal activation, allowing upregulation of beta-1 receptors and restoration of contractile reserve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-blockers directly reduce preload by blocking sodium reabsorption in the proximal tubule

choices.label: E
choices.text: Beta-blockers inhibit angiotensin-converting enzyme, reducing aldosterone-mediated fluid retention

explanation.summary: Beta-blockers are a cornerstone of heart failure therapy despite causing initial hemodynamic worsening. Their long-term benefit stems from reversing the toxic effects of chronic catecholamine excess on the failing myocardium.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic heart failure, sustained sympathetic activation leads to beta-1 receptor downregulation, cardiomyocyte apoptosis, and pathologic remodeling. Beta-blockers interrupt this cycle by shielding the myocardium from catecholamine toxicity, allowing beta-1 receptor upregulation (restoring adrenergic responsiveness), reducing myocyte apoptosis, and promoting reverse remodeling with improved contractile function over months. The initial worsening reflects the acute negative inotropic effect before these beneficial adaptations develop.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-blockers actually decrease (not increase) calcium release acutely by reducing cAMP-mediated PKA phosphorylation of L-type calcium channels and RyR2. The improved contractility seen chronically is due to receptor upregulation and reverse remodeling, not enhanced calcium release.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While heart rate reduction is one mechanism of benefit, it alone does not explain the improvement in EF and reverse remodeling. The primary long-term benefit is from reversal of neurohormonal toxicity, not simply improved diastolic filling time.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers have no direct effect on renal sodium handling. Diuretic effects are mediated by loop diuretics (blocking NKCC2), thiazides (blocking NCC), and aldosterone antagonists (blocking ENaC). Beta-blockers actually reduce renin release, indirectly reducing aldosterone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-blockers do not inhibit ACE. This describes the mechanism of ACE inhibitors (enalapril, lisinopril). While both drug classes improve outcomes in HF, they act through distinct mechanisms.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 22% collapses at home. Paramedics find him in ventricular fibrillation and successfully defibrillate him. After stabilization, coronary angiography reveals no obstructive coronary artery disease. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term therapy to reduce his risk of sudden cardiac death?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term oral amiodarone therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the arrhythmia focus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of flecainide for rhythm control

choices.label: E
choices.text: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation

explanation.summary: This patient is a survivor of sudden cardiac arrest due to ventricular fibrillation in the setting of non-ischemic DCM. An ICD is indicated for secondary prevention of sudden cardiac death and has a Class I recommendation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Survivors of cardiac arrest due to VF or hemodynamically unstable VT have a high risk of recurrence (30-40% within 2 years). Multiple trials (AVID, CIDS, CASH) have demonstrated that ICDs reduce mortality compared to antiarrhythmic drugs in secondary prevention. The ICD continuously monitors cardiac rhythm and delivers defibrillation within seconds of detecting a lethal arrhythmia, providing reliable protection against sudden cardiac death.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amiodarone reduces arrhythmia recurrence but has not consistently demonstrated mortality benefit compared to ICD in secondary prevention. The AVID trial showed ICD superiority over amiodarone for survival. Amiodarone also has significant long-term toxicities (pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid dysfunction, hepatotoxicity).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Catheter ablation may be used as adjunctive therapy for recurrent ventricular arrhythmias in DCM but is not a substitute for ICD implantation in secondary prevention. The diffuse myocardial substrate in DCM makes ablation less effective as a standalone therapy compared to focal arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Flecainide (Class IC antiarrhythmic) is contraindicated in patients with structural heart disease and reduced EF. The CAST trial demonstrated increased mortality with Class IC drugs in patients with structural heart disease due to proarrhythmic effects.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A dual-chamber pacemaker treats bradyarrhythmias and has no capability to treat ventricular fibrillation or tachycardia. This patient requires defibrillation capability, which only an ICD provides.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with recently diagnosed idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy has an echocardiogram showing an ejection fraction of 25%, an end-diastolic diameter of 72 mm, and moderate functional mitral regurgitation. The mitral valve leaflets are structurally normal. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of mitral regurgitation in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral annular dilation and papillary muscle displacement from ventricular remodeling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rheumatic thickening and commissural fusion of the mitral valve leaflets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myxomatous degeneration with excessive leaflet tissue and chordal elongation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Papillary muscle rupture following transmural myocardial infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Calcific degeneration of the mitral annulus restricting leaflet motion

explanation.summary: Functional (secondary) mitral regurgitation in DCM occurs because of ventricular remodeling that distorts the mitral valve apparatus without intrinsic valve pathology. The structurally normal leaflets confirm this is functional, not organic, MR.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DCM, progressive ventricular dilation causes mitral annular dilation (enlarging the orifice the leaflets must cover) and lateral/apical displacement of the papillary muscles (tethering the leaflets and preventing adequate coaptation). These geometric changes result in incomplete mitral leaflet closure during systole, causing regurgitation. The severity of functional MR correlates with the degree of LV dilation and sphericity index, and it can improve with reverse remodeling from medical therapy or CRT.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Rheumatic mitral disease causes thickened, immobile leaflets with commissural fusion, typically presenting with mitral stenosis (or mixed stenosis/regurgitation). The question specifies structurally normal leaflets, ruling out rheumatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myxomatous degeneration (mitral valve prolapse) is a primary valve disorder with excessive redundant tissue, chordal elongation, and leaflet prolapse into the atrium. This is organic (primary) MR, whereas this patient's MR is functional with structurally normal leaflets.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Papillary muscle rupture is a mechanical complication of acute MI causing acute severe MR with hemodynamic collapse. This patient has chronic DCM with moderate MR and no history of MI.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitral annular calcification restricts posterior leaflet motion and is seen in elderly patients or those with chronic kidney disease. It does not cause the type of functional MR seen with ventricular dilation in DCM.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 22-year-old college basketball player is referred for evaluation after his brother died suddenly during exercise at age 19. Physical examination reveals a harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the left sternal border that increases with standing from a squatting position and during the strain phase of Valsalva. Echocardiography shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy with a septal thickness of 22 mm, systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, and a resting left ventricular outflow tract gradient of 40 mmHg. Which of the following is the most likely genetic basis of this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autosomal dominant mutation in genes encoding sarcomeric proteins

choices.label: B
choices.text: X-linked recessive mutation in the dystrophin gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trinucleotide repeat expansion in the frataxin gene

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autosomal recessive mutation in the hemochromatosis gene (HFE)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitochondrial DNA deletion causing oxidative phosphorylation defects

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is the most common inherited cardiac disorder, with an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. It is caused by mutations in genes encoding sarcomeric proteins, most commonly beta-myosin heavy chain (MYH7) and myosin-binding protein C (MYBPC3).
explanation.whyCorrect: HCM is caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomeric protein genes with variable penetrance and expressivity. Over 1,500 mutations have been identified across at least 11 genes, with MYH7 and MYBPC3 accounting for approximately 70% of genotype-positive cases. These mutations cause disorganized myocyte architecture (myocardial disarray), asymmetric hypertrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and the characteristic dynamic LVOT obstruction seen in this patient. The family history of sudden death is consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Dystrophin gene mutations cause Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophy, which can cause a dilated cardiomyopathy, not the hypertrophic phenotype with asymmetric septal hypertrophy and LVOT obstruction seen here. Inheritance is X-linked recessive, affecting primarily males.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Trinucleotide repeat expansion in frataxin causes Friedreich ataxia, which can produce concentric (symmetric) LV hypertrophy but presents with progressive ataxia, scoliosis, and diabetes. It does not cause asymmetric septal hypertrophy or dynamic LVOT obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HFE mutations cause hereditary hemochromatosis, which leads to iron deposition in the myocardium causing restrictive or dilated cardiomyopathy, not hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. It presents with other features of iron overload including hepatic cirrhosis and diabetes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitochondrial DNA deletions can cause cardiomyopathy as part of mitochondrial myopathy syndromes (e.g., Kearns-Sayre syndrome), but these typically produce concentric hypertrophy or dilated cardiomyopathy with multi-system disease, not the classic HCM phenotype with asymmetric septal hypertrophy.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy has a resting LVOT gradient of 60 mmHg. His physician asks him to perform various maneuvers while monitoring the gradient by Doppler echocardiography. Which of the following maneuvers would be expected to decrease the LVOT gradient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Standing abruptly from a squatting position

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performing the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver

choices.label: C
choices.text: Passive leg elevation while supine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administration of sublingual nitroglycerin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Post-premature ventricular contraction beat

explanation.summary: The dynamic LVOT gradient in HCM is influenced by ventricular volume, contractility, and afterload. Maneuvers that increase preload (ventricular volume) decrease the gradient by widening the LVOT and reducing the Venturi effect that drives systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve.
explanation.whyCorrect: Passive leg elevation increases venous return and preload, distending the left ventricle. A larger LV cavity separates the hypertrophied septum from the mitral valve leaflets, reducing the Venturi effect that pulls the anterior mitral leaflet toward the septum (SAM). This decreases the dynamic LVOT obstruction. Other preload-increasing maneuvers (squatting, volume infusion) have similar effects. This is the opposite response to what occurs in most murmurs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Standing abruptly decreases venous return (blood pools in the lower extremities), reducing preload and LV cavity size. This brings the septum and mitral leaflet closer together, increasing the Venturi effect and worsening the LVOT gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The strain phase of Valsalva reduces venous return by increasing intrathoracic pressure, which decreases preload and LV volume. This increases the LVOT gradient by the same mechanism as standing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroglycerin is a venodilator that decreases preload through venous pooling, and it also reduces afterload. Both effects decrease LV volume and increase the LVOT gradient. Nitroglycerin is contraindicated in obstructive HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The post-PVC beat has enhanced contractility (post-extrasystolic potentiation) and follows a compensatory pause that increases LV filling. However, the increased contractility dominates, causing a net increase in the LVOT gradient. This is the Brockenbrough-Braunwald-Morrow sign used to diagnose HOCM.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 25-year history of hypertension (blood pressure consistently 160/100 mmHg) presents for routine evaluation. Echocardiography reveals concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with a wall thickness of 14 mm and a normal ejection fraction. Which of the following cellular mechanisms is most directly responsible for the development of concentric hypertrophy in response to chronic pressure overload?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addition of new sarcomeres in parallel, increasing myocyte cross-sectional area

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperplasia of cardiomyocytes increasing total cell number

choices.label: C
choices.text: Addition of new sarcomeres in series, lengthening the myocyte

choices.label: D
choices.text: Replacement of cardiomyocytes with collagen and fibroblasts

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recruitment of cardiac progenitor cells to the hypertrophied wall

explanation.summary: Concentric left ventricular hypertrophy from chronic pressure overload results from parallel addition of sarcomeres within individual cardiomyocytes, increasing cell width and wall thickness without chamber dilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: According to the law of Laplace (wall stress = pressure × radius / 2 × wall thickness), chronic pressure overload increases systolic wall stress. Cardiomyocytes respond by adding sarcomeres in parallel, increasing their cross-sectional area and wall thickness. This normalizes wall stress by increasing the denominator. The result is concentric hypertrophy with increased wall thickness relative to chamber size (increased relative wall thickness), preserving systolic function initially.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Adult cardiomyocytes are terminally differentiated and have extremely limited capacity for hyperplasia (cell division). Cardiac hypertrophy occurs through hypertrophy (cell enlargement) of existing myocytes, not through generation of new cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Addition of sarcomeres in series (lengthening the myocyte) occurs in response to volume overload (e.g., aortic regurgitation, mitral regurgitation), producing eccentric hypertrophy with chamber dilation. Pressure overload produces the opposite response: parallel addition with wall thickening.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrosis and collagen deposition occur as a secondary process in chronic hypertrophy but are not the primary mechanism of wall thickening. Replacement fibrosis is a maladaptive response that impairs diastolic function and occurs later in the disease process.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While cardiac progenitor cells have been identified, their contribution to myocardial mass in adult hypertrophy is negligible. The overwhelming mechanism of increased wall thickness is hypertrophy of existing terminally differentiated cardiomyocytes.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with known hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents with exertional dyspnea. Echocardiography shows a septal thickness of 28 mm with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve and a resting LVOT gradient of 75 mmHg. She has failed maximal doses of metoprolol and verapamil. Her physician recommends septal reduction therapy. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which alcohol septal ablation reduces the LVOT gradient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alcohol causes vasodilation of the septal perforator arteries, reducing myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: B
choices.text: Injection of alcohol into a septal perforator artery creates a controlled septal infarction, thinning the obstructing septum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alcohol directly relaxes hypertrophied septal myocytes, reducing their contractility

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alcohol induces fibrosis of the mitral valve leaflets, preventing systolic anterior motion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alcohol ablation causes selective denervation of the septum, reducing adrenergic-driven hypertrophy

explanation.summary: Alcohol septal ablation (ASA) is a percutaneous alternative to surgical myectomy for drug-refractory obstructive HCM. It involves injection of absolute ethanol into a septal perforator artery to create a targeted myocardial infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: During ASA, a catheter is positioned in the first or second septal perforator artery of the left anterior descending coronary artery. Injection of 1-3 mL of absolute ethanol causes a controlled infarction of the basal septum at the site of obstruction. Over weeks to months, the infarcted tissue undergoes fibrosis and thinning, widening the LVOT and reducing the dynamic gradient. Echocardiographic contrast injection is used to confirm the target area before alcohol delivery. The gradient reduction is comparable to surgical myectomy in experienced centers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alcohol does not cause therapeutic vasodilation of septal arteries. It is a fixative that causes protein denaturation, cell membrane disruption, and coagulative necrosis of the myocardium supplied by the injected artery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alcohol does not selectively relax myocytes. It causes irreversible cell death through protein denaturation and direct cytotoxicity. The therapeutic effect comes from tissue destruction and subsequent remodeling, not modulation of contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alcohol septal ablation does not affect the mitral valve leaflets directly. The resolution of SAM occurs secondary to widening of the LVOT after septal thinning, which reduces the Venturi forces that pull the anterior leaflet toward the septum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While some sympathetic denervation may occur in the infarcted zone, this is not the primary mechanism of gradient reduction. The mechanism is mechanical: physical thinning of the obstructing septum to widen the LVOT.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 19-year-old man collapses during a soccer match and is pronounced dead at the scene. Autopsy reveals a heart weight of 580 g, asymmetric septal hypertrophy (26 mm), and extensive myocardial disarray with interstitial fibrosis. There is no coronary artery disease. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of sudden cardiac death in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute aortic dissection from elevated left ventricular pressures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete atrioventricular block from fibrosis of the conduction system

choices.label: C
choices.text: Massive pulmonary embolism from deep venous thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery spasm causing acute transmural myocardial infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular fibrillation arising from the disorganized myocardial substrate

explanation.summary: HCM is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the United States. The mechanism is ventricular fibrillation arising from the arrhythmogenic substrate of myocardial disarray, fibrosis, and abnormal intramural coronary arteries.
explanation.whyCorrect: The disorganized myocardial architecture (disarray) in HCM creates heterogeneous conduction pathways with variable refractory periods, forming the ideal substrate for re-entrant ventricular arrhythmias. During exercise, the combination of increased catecholamines, increased LVOT gradient, myocardial ischemia (from supply-demand mismatch in hypertrophied muscle with abnormal intramural arteries), and the abnormal substrate triggers ventricular tachycardia degenerating into ventricular fibrillation. Interstitial fibrosis further disrupts cell-to-cell electrical coupling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic dissection is associated with connective tissue disorders (Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos) and severe hypertension, not HCM. The autopsy findings of myocardial disarray and asymmetric septal hypertrophy are diagnostic of HCM, not aortic pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While conduction abnormalities can occur in HCM, complete heart block is rare and is not the typical mechanism of sudden death in young athletes with HCM. The primary mechanism is ventricular tachyarrhythmia, not bradyarrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary embolism would show right ventricular dilation and clot in the pulmonary arteries at autopsy. The findings here are entirely consistent with HCM, and PE is not associated with the described pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The autopsy specifically notes no coronary artery disease. Coronary spasm causes transmural ischemia in a coronary distribution, which is a different mechanism. In HCM, myocardial ischemia occurs from supply-demand mismatch and small vessel disease, not epicardial coronary spasm.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents with recurrent exertional syncope. His resting LVOT gradient is 35 mmHg, which increases to 90 mmHg with provocation. Echocardiography shows systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with posteriorly directed mitral regurgitation. Which of the following hemodynamic findings on arterial pressure tracing is most characteristic of obstructive HCM?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulsus paradoxus with >10 mmHg inspiratory decline in systolic pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulsus parvus et tardus with slow-rising, low-amplitude carotid upstroke

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bisferiens pulse with a spike-and-dome arterial waveform

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wide pulse pressure with bounding peripheral pulses

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulsus alternans with alternating strong and weak beats

explanation.summary: The bisferiens (spike-and-dome) pulse is the classic arterial waveform finding in obstructive HCM. It reflects the dynamic nature of the LVOT obstruction during systole.
explanation.whyCorrect: In obstructive HCM, the arterial waveform shows two systolic peaks. The initial spike represents rapid early ejection before the onset of LVOT obstruction (when the outflow tract is still relatively open). As systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve develops mid-systole, the LVOT narrows, causing a transient decrease in forward flow (the 'notch'). The second dome represents continued ejection against the obstruction. This spike-and-dome morphology is pathognomonic for dynamic LVOT obstruction in HCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulsus paradoxus (exaggerated inspiratory decline in systolic pressure) is seen in cardiac tamponade, severe asthma, and constrictive pericarditis, not HCM. It reflects exaggerated interventricular interdependence.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, late-peaking pulse) is the classic arterial finding in severe aortic stenosis, reflecting fixed obstruction. In HCM, the obstruction is dynamic with initial rapid ejection, producing a brisk rather than slow upstroke.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Wide pulse pressure with bounding pulses (Watson's water-hammer pulse) is characteristic of aortic regurgitation, where rapid diastolic runoff increases the difference between systolic and diastolic pressures. HCM does not cause this pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulsus alternans (alternating strong and weak beats at regular intervals) is a sign of severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction and impending cardiogenic shock. It is not associated with the preserved EF seen in HCM.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is evaluated for sudden cardiac death risk stratification. He has a maximal wall thickness of 32 mm, no family history of sudden death, no prior syncope, no nonsustained ventricular tachycardia on Holter monitoring, and a normal blood pressure response to exercise. Cardiac MRI shows extensive late gadolinium enhancement involving 20% of the myocardium. Based on current risk factors, which single finding in this patient most strongly supports ICD implantation for primary prevention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age less than 40 years at diagnosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resting LVOT gradient greater than 30 mmHg

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maximal wall thickness of 32 mm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Male sex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Presence of systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve

explanation.summary: Massive left ventricular hypertrophy (maximal wall thickness ≥30 mm) is one of the established major risk factors for sudden cardiac death in HCM and is an independent indication for ICD consideration for primary prevention.
explanation.whyCorrect: A maximal wall thickness ≥30 mm is one of the conventional major risk factors for sudden cardiac death in HCM, along with family history of SCD, unexplained syncope, nonsustained VT, and abnormal blood pressure response to exercise. Massive hypertrophy is associated with a significantly increased risk of fatal arrhythmias, likely due to greater myocardial disarray, more extensive fibrosis (reflected by the extensive LGE on MRI), and increased propensity for ischemia. Current guidelines recommend that the presence of even one major risk factor warrants discussion of ICD implantation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While younger age at diagnosis may suggest a more aggressive genetic substrate, age alone is not one of the established major risk factors for SCD in HCM risk stratification models.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A resting LVOT gradient >30 mmHg contributes to the overall risk profile and is included in newer risk calculators (e.g., HCM Risk-SCD), but among the options listed, the massive wall thickness of 32 mm is the single most compelling independent risk factor supporting ICD consideration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Male sex is not an established major risk factor for sudden cardiac death in HCM. SCD risk stratification in HCM is based on specific clinical markers, not sex alone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SAM of the mitral valve is a mechanism of LVOT obstruction and is common in obstructive HCM but is not an independent major risk factor for SCD. The gradient severity, not SAM itself, contributes to risk assessment.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents to the emergency department with acute pulmonary edema after receiving a medication at an urgent care clinic for a suspected urinary tract infection. His LVOT gradient, previously 45 mmHg, is now measured at 110 mmHg. Which of the following medications most likely precipitated this acute decompensation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous ciprofloxacin with concurrent oral phenazopyridine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous normal saline bolus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral metoprolol tartrate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole infused with a large volume of normal saline

explanation.summary: Ciprofloxacin can prolong the QT interval and, more importantly in this context, IV administration with its associated vasodilation can reduce afterload and preload, worsening LVOT obstruction. However, the most significant factor is that many IV formulations require volume depletion awareness, and fluoroquinolones can cause vasodilation that reduces preload and afterload, exacerbating dynamic LVOT obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intravenous ciprofloxacin administration is associated with vasodilation and hypotension, which can reduce afterload and venous return. In obstructive HCM, any decrease in preload or afterload worsens the dynamic LVOT gradient by reducing LV cavity size and increasing the Venturi effect. Additionally, phenazopyridine has no protective cardiovascular effects. The combination of IV-associated vasodilation from ciprofloxacin with a patient who may already be volume-depleted from a UTI creates conditions that dramatically increase the LVOT gradient.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Intravenous normal saline increases preload and intravascular volume, which would decrease (not increase) the LVOT gradient. Volume resuscitation is actually a therapeutic maneuver for acute LVOT obstruction in HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Metoprolol is a first-line therapy for obstructive HCM. Beta-blockers decrease heart rate, reduce contractility, and prolong diastolic filling time, all of which decrease the LVOT gradient. Metoprolol would improve, not worsen, this patient's hemodynamics.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amoxicillin-clavulanate does not have significant hemodynamic effects and would not alter the LVOT gradient. It is a safe antibiotic choice in patients with obstructive HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole infused with a large volume of normal saline would increase preload from the volume infusion, which would decrease the LVOT gradient. TMP-SMX itself does not cause significant hemodynamic changes that would worsen obstruction.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a 30-year history of uncontrolled hypertension presents with exertional dyspnea. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with a strain pattern. Echocardiography reveals concentric LVH with a wall thickness of 15 mm, an EF of 60%, and Grade II diastolic dysfunction. Which of the following pathophysiologic consequences of LVH most directly explains this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced myocardial oxygen supply due to decreased capillary density relative to myocyte mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: Systolic dysfunction from progressive myocyte death and sarcomere loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction from asymmetric septal hypertrophy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right ventricular failure from chronic pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated left ventricular filling pressures from impaired relaxation and reduced compliance

explanation.summary: In hypertensive LVH with preserved EF, exertional dyspnea is caused by diastolic dysfunction. The thickened, stiff ventricle has impaired relaxation and reduced compliance, leading to elevated filling pressures that are transmitted to the pulmonary vasculature.
explanation.whyCorrect: Concentric LVH causes diastolic dysfunction through two mechanisms: (1) impaired active relaxation (lusitropy) due to delayed calcium reuptake by SERCA2a in hypertrophied myocytes, and (2) reduced passive compliance from increased interstitial fibrosis and increased myocyte stiffness. The Grade II diastolic dysfunction (pseudonormalized filling pattern) indicates elevated left atrial pressures. During exertion, the inability to adequately fill the stiff ventricle causes further elevation of pulmonary venous pressures, producing dyspnea.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While reduced capillary density relative to myocyte mass does occur in LVH (capillary rarefaction) and can cause subendocardial ischemia, this contributes to anginal symptoms rather than directly explaining exertional dyspnea. The primary symptom mechanism is diastolic dysfunction with elevated filling pressures.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This patient has a preserved EF of 60%, indicating intact systolic function. Systolic dysfunction with sarcomere loss describes decompensated (burned-out) hypertensive cardiomyopathy, which occurs in late-stage disease with declining EF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dynamic LVOT obstruction is characteristic of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) with asymmetric septal hypertrophy, not the symmetric concentric LVH of hypertensive heart disease. This patient has concentric hypertrophy without LVOT obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While chronic elevation of LV filling pressures can eventually cause pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure, this is a late complication. The patient's symptoms are most directly explained by left-sided diastolic dysfunction, not primary right ventricular failure.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with obstructive HCM is started on a new medication. After 8 weeks, his LVOT gradient has decreased from 65 mmHg to 18 mmHg, and his exercise capacity has improved significantly. The medication's mechanism of action involves direct inhibition of cardiac myosin ATPase activity. Which of the following drugs was most likely prescribed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disopyramide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diltiazem

choices.label: C
choices.text: Propranolol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mavacamten

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ranolazine

explanation.summary: Mavacamten is a first-in-class cardiac myosin inhibitor approved for the treatment of symptomatic obstructive HCM. It directly targets the underlying pathophysiology by reducing excessive myosin-actin cross-bridge formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mavacamten selectively inhibits cardiac myosin ATPase, reducing the number of actin-myosin cross-bridges that can form during systole. This decreases excessive contractility, which is the fundamental problem in HCM. By reducing hypercontractility, mavacamten decreases the LVOT gradient, reduces SAM of the mitral valve, and improves symptoms. The EXPLORER-HCM trial demonstrated significant reduction in LVOT gradients and improvement in functional capacity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Disopyramide is a Class IA antiarrhythmic that reduces LVOT gradient through its negative inotropic effects (sodium channel blockade reducing contractility), but its mechanism is not direct myosin ATPase inhibition. It also has significant anticholinergic side effects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that reduces LVOT gradient by decreasing heart rate and contractility through L-type calcium channel blockade, not direct myosin inhibition. It is a second-line agent for HCM when beta-blockers are contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that reduces LVOT gradient by decreasing heart rate and contractility through beta-adrenergic receptor blockade. It acts upstream of myosin, reducing cAMP-mediated calcium entry, not by directly inhibiting myosin ATPase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ranolazine inhibits the late sodium current and is used for chronic angina. It does not directly inhibit cardiac myosin ATPase and is not an approved treatment for obstructive HCM.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with known non-obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents with progressive dyspnea and fatigue over the past year. Echocardiography now shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 30%, compared to 70% two years ago. The maximal wall thickness has decreased from 20 mm to 13 mm. Which of the following best describes this patient's current condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Typical progression of HCM with increasing hypertrophy and outflow obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development of takotsubo cardiomyopathy superimposed on HCM

choices.label: C
choices.text: Burned-out phase of HCM with transition to a dilated cardiomyopathy phenotype

choices.label: D
choices.text: New-onset viral myocarditis unrelated to the underlying HCM

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iatrogenic cardiomyopathy from excessive beta-blocker use

explanation.summary: The 'burned-out' or end-stage phase of HCM occurs in approximately 5-10% of patients and is characterized by progressive wall thinning, LV dilation, and systolic dysfunction, mimicking dilated cardiomyopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: End-stage (burned-out) HCM results from progressive myocyte death, replacement fibrosis, and ventricular remodeling. The hallmarks are: decrease in wall thickness (from 20 mm to 13 mm in this case), LV cavity dilation, and declining EF (from 70% to 30%). This represents a transition from the hypertrophic phenotype to a dilated phenotype and carries a poor prognosis. These patients often require heart failure therapy and may need transplant evaluation. The extensive fibrosis that replaces hypertrophied myocardium drives the progressive systolic dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Typical HCM progression would show stable or increasing hypertrophy, not wall thinning and dilation. This patient shows the opposite: decreasing wall thickness with declining EF, which is the burned-out phase rather than typical disease progression.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy presents acutely with characteristic apical ballooning following emotional or physical stress and is typically reversible within weeks. This patient's progressive decline over a year with wall thinning is inconsistent with takotsubo.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While viral myocarditis could cause new systolic dysfunction, the progressive wall thinning from 20 mm to 13 mm over years in a patient with known HCM is characteristic of end-stage HCM, not a coincidental acute viral process.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-blockers can reduce LVOT gradient and improve symptoms but do not cause progressive wall thinning or the degree of systolic dysfunction seen here. Beta-blocker-induced cardiomyopathy is exceptionally rare, and the pattern of wall thinning with dilation in known HCM points to the burned-out phase.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with long-standing hypertension presents for evaluation of an ECG that shows tall R waves in V5-V6, deep S waves in V1-V2, and ST depression with T-wave inversions in the lateral leads. The Sokolow-Lyon voltage criterion (S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 > 35 mm) is met. Which of the following pathologic changes in the hypertrophied myocardium best explains the ST-T wave changes (strain pattern) seen on this ECG?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute transmural ischemia from complete coronary artery occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Subendocardial ischemia and repolarization abnormalities from increased wall stress and oxygen demand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pericardial inflammation causing diffuse ST elevation with PR depression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperkalemia causing peaked T waves and widened QRS complexes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bundle branch block causing discordant ST-T changes

explanation.summary: The LVH strain pattern (ST depression with asymmetric T-wave inversion in lateral leads) reflects chronic subendocardial ischemia and abnormal repolarization in the hypertrophied myocardium.
explanation.whyCorrect: In LVH, the subendocardium is most vulnerable to ischemia because: (1) increased wall stress from hypertrophy raises myocardial oxygen demand; (2) the subendocardium receives blood flow during diastole, and the shortened diastolic time in hypertrophied hearts reduces perfusion; (3) capillary rarefaction (decreased capillary-to-myocyte ratio) limits oxygen delivery; (4) extravascular compression from elevated intramural pressures further compromises subendocardial flow. These factors cause chronic subendocardial ischemia, altering the repolarization sequence and producing the characteristic downsloping ST depression with asymmetric T-wave inversions (strain pattern).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute transmural ischemia from complete coronary occlusion produces ST elevation in the territory of the affected artery (STEMI pattern), not the diffuse lateral ST depression with T-wave inversions seen with LVH strain. The strain pattern is chronic and present at baseline.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pericarditis produces diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, which is morphologically distinct from the lateral ST depression and T-wave inversion of LVH strain. There is no clinical suggestion of pericarditis in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hyperkalemia produces peaked, symmetric T waves and progressive QRS widening, not the asymmetric T-wave inversions with ST depression characteristic of the LVH strain pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While bundle branch blocks cause secondary ST-T changes discordant to the QRS axis, this ECG shows LVH voltage criteria without mention of QRS prolongation or bundle branch block morphology. The strain pattern is directly attributable to the LVH itself.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy asks about the safety of becoming pregnant. Her current echocardiogram shows a septal thickness of 18 mm, an LVOT gradient of 25 mmHg at rest that increases to 55 mmHg with Valsalva, and an EF of 72%. She is asymptomatic on metoprolol. Which of the following physiologic changes during pregnancy is most likely to worsen her LVOT obstruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased blood volume and preload during the second trimester

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased red blood cell mass improving oxygen delivery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Physiologic bradycardia from enhanced vagal tone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased systemic vascular resistance throughout pregnancy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased progesterone levels reducing cardiac contractility

explanation.summary: Pregnancy causes a significant decrease in systemic vascular resistance (SVR) due to hormonal effects (progesterone, relaxin, prostacyclin) and the low-resistance placental circulation. Decreased afterload worsens LVOT obstruction in HCM.
explanation.whyCorrect: Decreased SVR reduces afterload, which decreases the pressure that opposes ventricular ejection. In obstructive HCM, reduced afterload allows more rapid early ejection, increasing flow velocity through the narrowed LVOT. This enhances the Venturi effect, pulling the mitral valve leaflet toward the septum (worsening SAM) and increasing the dynamic gradient. The 25-40% decrease in SVR during pregnancy is the physiologic change most likely to exacerbate LVOT obstruction, particularly during delivery when SVR may drop further.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased blood volume and preload during pregnancy would actually help reduce the LVOT gradient by expanding the LV cavity and separating the septum from the mitral valve leaflet. This is a protective hemodynamic change in obstructive HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased red blood cell mass improves oxygen-carrying capacity but does not significantly affect LVOT hemodynamics. The RBC mass increase (approximately 30%) is also relatively less than the plasma volume expansion (approximately 50%), resulting in physiologic anemia of pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pregnancy is characterized by an increase in resting heart rate (approximately 10-20 bpm above baseline), not bradycardia. Additionally, physiologic bradycardia would prolong diastolic filling time, which would increase preload and potentially decrease the LVOT gradient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While progesterone has some smooth muscle relaxation effects contributing to decreased SVR, it does not directly reduce cardiac contractility. In fact, pregnancy is associated with increased cardiac output from both increased heart rate and increased stroke volume.

questions.topicName: Left Ventricular Hypertrophy and Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
questions.stem: A 33-year-old man undergoes cardiac catheterization for evaluation of suspected hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. A catheter is advanced retrograde across the aortic valve into the left ventricle. Simultaneous pressure recordings from the LV apex and the subaortic region show a 70 mmHg systolic pressure gradient within the left ventricle. After a premature ventricular contraction is induced, the post-PVC beat shows an increased LV-aortic pressure gradient but a decreased aortic pulse pressure. Which of the following best explains this hemodynamic response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fixed obstruction at the aortic valve level, as seen in calcific aortic stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dynamic subaortic obstruction with enhanced contractility worsening the gradient (Brockenbrough-Braunwald-Morrow sign)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Severe mitral regurgitation with reduced forward cardiac output

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiac tamponade with impaired ventricular filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Complete heart block with inadequate ventricular rate

explanation.summary: The Brockenbrough-Braunwald-Morrow sign is a pathognomonic hemodynamic finding in obstructive HCM. After a PVC, the post-extrasystolic beat has increased contractility but paradoxically shows a decrease in aortic pulse pressure, which is the opposite of what occurs in aortic stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: After a PVC, the compensatory pause allows greater ventricular filling, and post-extrasystolic potentiation increases contractility. In fixed aortic stenosis, this increased contractility increases both the transvalvular gradient and the aortic pulse pressure. However, in obstructive HCM, the enhanced contractility worsens the dynamic LVOT obstruction disproportionately, so although the LV generates more pressure, the increased obstruction prevents forward flow into the aorta, paradoxically decreasing the aortic pulse pressure. This dissociation between increased LV pressure and decreased aortic pressure is the Brockenbrough-Braunwald-Morrow sign.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In fixed aortic stenosis, the post-PVC beat increases both the transvalvular gradient AND the aortic pulse pressure proportionally, because the obstruction does not change with contractility. The paradoxical decrease in aortic pulse pressure distinguishes dynamic obstruction (HCM) from fixed obstruction (AS).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Severe mitral regurgitation would decrease forward output but would not produce the specific pattern of an intracavitary gradient with paradoxical post-PVC response. MR would be reflected in a large V wave on the PCWP tracing, not this specific hemodynamic signature.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac tamponade causes equalization of diastolic pressures across all chambers and pulsus paradoxus, not an intracavitary systolic pressure gradient within the left ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Complete heart block would produce a slow ventricular rate and cannon A waves on JVP but would not cause an intracavitary pressure gradient or the specific post-PVC hemodynamic response described.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive exertional dyspnea, lower extremity edema, and early satiety over the past 8 months. He has a 20-year history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals jugular venous distension that increases with inspiration, hepatomegaly, and bilateral pitting edema. Echocardiography shows biatrial enlargement, normal left ventricular cavity size, a wall thickness of 14 mm, and an ejection fraction of 58%. Doppler analysis demonstrates a restrictive filling pattern with an E/A ratio of 3.2. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with left ventricular outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constrictive pericarditis due to chronic inflammatory pericardial thickening

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac amyloidosis from chronic systemic AA amyloid deposition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy secondary to chronic inflammatory myocarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy with apical ballooning and wall motion abnormalities

explanation.summary: This patient with longstanding rheumatoid arthritis presents with signs of biventricular heart failure, increased wall thickness with normal cavity size, preserved EF, and a restrictive filling pattern. The combination of chronic inflammatory disease and infiltrative-appearing cardiomyopathy strongly suggests AA amyloidosis with cardiac involvement.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis are the most common cause of systemic AA amyloidosis, in which serum amyloid A protein deposits in organs including the heart. Cardiac amyloidosis causes restrictive cardiomyopathy with increased wall thickness (due to amyloid infiltration, not true hypertrophy), preserved or mildly reduced EF, biatrial enlargement, and a restrictive Doppler filling pattern (elevated E/A ratio). The Kussmaul sign (JVD increasing with inspiration) reflects impaired ventricular filling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy causes asymmetric septal hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction but typically presents with dynamic outflow obstruction, systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, and a crescendo-decrescendo murmur. It is a genetic sarcomeric disease, not associated with chronic inflammatory conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Constrictive pericarditis can mimic restrictive cardiomyopathy with similar hemodynamics and Kussmaul sign. However, it shows pericardial thickening or calcification, ventricular interdependence on imaging, and normal wall thickness. The increased wall thickness of 14 mm strongly favors an infiltrative myocardial process.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dilated cardiomyopathy is characterized by increased ventricular cavity size and reduced ejection fraction. This patient has a normal cavity size and preserved EF, which is inconsistent with a dilated phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy presents acutely after emotional or physical stress with transient apical wall motion abnormalities and reduced EF. It does not cause increased wall thickness, biatrial enlargement, or a restrictive filling pattern.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of multiple myeloma presents with fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and syncope. ECG shows low-voltage QRS complexes and a pseudo-infarction pattern with Q waves in the anterior leads. Echocardiography reveals concentrically thickened ventricular walls with a granular sparkling appearance, biatrial enlargement, and an ejection fraction of 50%. Serum free light chain assay shows markedly elevated lambda light chains. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of the ECG finding of low-voltage QRS complexes in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replacement of normal myocardium with electrically inert amyloid fibrils reducing total electrical mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased pericardial fluid attenuating electrical signals before reaching the body surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diffuse subendocardial ischemia causing transmural conduction delay and voltage loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extensive myocardial fibrosis from repeated episodes of viral myocarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Severe left ventricular hypertrophy causing cancellation of opposing electrical vectors

explanation.summary: This patient with multiple myeloma and elevated lambda light chains has AL (light-chain) amyloidosis with cardiac involvement. The paradox of increased wall thickness on echocardiography with low-voltage QRS on ECG is a hallmark of cardiac amyloidosis. Amyloid fibrils infiltrate and replace normal myocardium, reducing electrically active tissue mass.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cardiac amyloidosis, extracellular deposition of amyloid fibrils between and around cardiomyocytes displaces functioning myocardium. Although the walls appear thickened on echocardiography (due to amyloid deposition), the actual mass of electrically active myocardium is reduced. These inert amyloid deposits do not generate electrical activity, resulting in low-voltage QRS complexes despite the echocardiographic appearance of increased wall thickness. This voltage-mass mismatch is nearly pathognomonic for cardiac amyloidosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pericardial effusion can cause low-voltage QRS complexes by attenuating signal transmission. However, this patient's echocardiogram describes thickened walls and biatrial enlargement without mention of pericardial effusion, and the granular sparkling pattern and light chain elevation point to infiltrative disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subendocardial ischemia can cause ST-segment depression but does not produce low-voltage QRS complexes. The pseudo-infarction pattern in amyloidosis results from amyloid deposition mimicking myocardial scar, not from ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Myocardial fibrosis from myocarditis could reduce voltages, but this patient has no history of myocarditis. The clinical picture of myeloma, elevated light chains, and the characteristic sparkling echocardiographic appearance point specifically to amyloid infiltration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: True left ventricular hypertrophy typically causes high-voltage QRS complexes, not low voltage. In amyloidosis, the wall thickening is from amyloid infiltration rather than myocyte hypertrophy, which is why the voltage is paradoxically low.
