questions.stem: A 45-year-old African American woman presents with progressive dyspnea, palpitations, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Echocardiography reveals mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function with an ejection fraction of 45%, basal septal thinning, and diastolic dysfunction with a restrictive filling pattern. Cardiac MRI demonstrates patchy areas of late gadolinium enhancement in the basal septum and lateral wall. Endomyocardial biopsy shows noncaseating granulomas. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methotrexate to suppress granulomatous inflammation in the myocardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-dose systemic corticosteroids to reduce myocardial granulomatous inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Infliximab to block tumor necrosis factor-alpha driven granuloma formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Azathioprine to prevent progression of fibrotic myocardial remodeling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mycophenolate mofetil to inhibit lymphocyte proliferation within granulomas

explanation.summary: This patient has cardiac sarcoidosis, evidenced by hilar lymphadenopathy, noncaseating granulomas on biopsy, late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI, and restrictive physiology. First-line treatment for cardiac sarcoidosis is high-dose systemic corticosteroids to suppress the granulomatous inflammatory process.
explanation.whyCorrect: Corticosteroids are the cornerstone of initial therapy for cardiac sarcoidosis. They reduce active granulomatous inflammation, may improve conduction abnormalities and ventricular function, and can prevent progression to irreversible fibrosis. A typical regimen begins with prednisone 40-60 mg/day, tapered over months based on clinical and imaging response.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing agent used as second-line therapy in sarcoidosis when corticosteroids are insufficient, cause intolerable side effects, or the patient relapses during steroid taper. It is not appropriate as initial monotherapy for cardiac sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Infliximab is reserved for refractory cardiac sarcoidosis that does not respond to corticosteroids and conventional immunosuppressants. TNF-alpha inhibitors carry their own risks and are not first-line therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Azathioprine is used as a steroid-sparing agent in combination with or following corticosteroids but is not recommended as initial standalone therapy for cardiac sarcoidosis due to its slower onset of action.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mycophenolate mofetil is another second-line immunosuppressive option for steroid-refractory or steroid-dependent sarcoidosis. It is not the initial treatment choice when active granulomatous myocardial inflammation is present.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a history of repeated blood transfusions for beta-thalassemia major presents with exertional dyspnea and pedal edema. Laboratory studies show a serum ferritin level of 4,500 ng/mL and transferrin saturation of 92%. Echocardiography reveals biventricular wall thickening, mildly dilated ventricles, and an ejection fraction of 38%. Cardiac MRI shows markedly reduced T2* values of 8 ms (normal >20 ms) throughout both ventricles. Which of the following pathologic findings would most likely be seen on endomyocardial biopsy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Noncaseating granulomas composed of epithelioid macrophages and giant cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Interstitial deposition of eosinophilic, Congo red-positive fibrillar material

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intracellular iron deposits within cardiomyocytes staining positive with Prussian blue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endomyocardial fibrosis with overlying mural thrombus formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Myocyte disarray with interstitial fibrosis and thickened intramural arterioles

explanation.summary: This patient with transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia has secondary hemochromatosis with cardiac iron overload. The markedly elevated ferritin, high transferrin saturation, and reduced T2* on cardiac MRI confirm iron deposition. Biopsy would show intracellular iron within cardiomyocytes, identifiable with Prussian blue staining.
explanation.whyCorrect: In transfusional iron overload, excess iron is taken up by cardiomyocytes through L-type calcium channels and divalent metal transporter 1. The iron accumulates as ferritin and hemosiderin within the sarcoplasm. Prussian blue (Perls) staining reveals blue-staining intracellular iron deposits. Cardiac MRI T2* values below 10 ms indicate severe iron loading and high risk of heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Noncaseating granulomas are characteristic of cardiac sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis presents with conduction abnormalities, patchy late gadolinium enhancement, and hilar lymphadenopathy, none of which are features in this transfusion-dependent patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Congo red-positive fibrillar material with apple-green birefringence under polarized light is diagnostic of amyloidosis. This patient's presentation with transfusion history, markedly elevated ferritin, and low T2* values on MRI points to iron overload, not amyloid deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Endomyocardial fibrosis with mural thrombus is seen in Loeffler endocarditis (hypereosinophilic syndrome) or tropical endomyocardial fibrosis. This patient has no eosinophilia or tropical exposure, and the clinical picture is consistent with iron overload cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Myocyte disarray with abnormal intramural arterioles is the hallmark histologic finding of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, a genetic sarcomeric disorder. This patient's clinical and laboratory profile is entirely consistent with secondary iron overload.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with heart failure symptoms. Echocardiography shows biatrial enlargement, increased biventricular wall thickness, and restrictive diastolic filling. Cardiac catheterization reveals equalization of diastolic pressures across all four chambers. The physician is trying to differentiate between restrictive cardiomyopathy and constrictive pericarditis. Which of the following hemodynamic findings during cardiac catheterization would most strongly favor a diagnosis of restrictive cardiomyopathy over constrictive pericarditis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A prominent y descent in the right atrial pressure tracing

choices.label: B
choices.text: A dip-and-plateau pattern in the ventricular pressure tracing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated right atrial pressure with a rapid x descent

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right ventricular end-diastolic pressure exceeding one-third of the right ventricular systolic pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left ventricular end-diastolic pressure exceeding right ventricular end-diastolic pressure by more than 5 mmHg

explanation.summary: Differentiating restrictive cardiomyopathy from constrictive pericarditis is a classic clinical challenge. A key distinguishing hemodynamic finding is that in restrictive cardiomyopathy, the left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) typically exceeds the right ventricular end-diastolic pressure (RVEDP) by more than 5 mmHg, whereas in constrictive pericarditis, diastolic pressures are nearly equalized across all chambers.
explanation.whyCorrect: In restrictive cardiomyopathy, the disease process often affects the left ventricle more than the right, resulting in higher left-sided filling pressures. An LVEDP-RVEDP gradient exceeding 5 mmHg strongly favors restriction over constriction. In constrictive pericarditis, the rigid pericardium equally constrains both ventricles, resulting in near-complete equalization of diastolic pressures (typically within 5 mmHg).

whyWrongByChoice.A: A prominent y descent reflects rapid early diastolic filling and is observed in both constrictive pericarditis and restrictive cardiomyopathy. In fact, the y descent may be even more prominent in constriction due to the abrupt cessation of filling.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The dip-and-plateau (square root sign) pattern reflects rapid early filling followed by an abrupt halt and is present in both restrictive cardiomyopathy and constrictive pericarditis. It is not useful for differentiating between the two.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated right atrial pressure with a rapid x descent can occur in both conditions. The x descent reflects atrial relaxation and is not a distinguishing hemodynamic feature between restriction and constriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An RVEDP exceeding one-third of RVSP is seen in both restrictive cardiomyopathy and constrictive pericarditis and therefore does not reliably differentiate between the two conditions.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with systemic AL amyloidosis and cardiac involvement is evaluated for worsening heart failure. His current medications include furosemide and spironolactone. His blood pressure is 95/60 mmHg, and he has recurrent near-syncope with exertion. The physician considers adding a medication to improve his symptoms. Which of the following drugs should be specifically avoided in this patient due to the risk of profound hemodynamic deterioration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low-dose metolazone to augment diuresis in the distal convoluted tubule

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digoxin for rate control and augmentation of cardiac contractility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cautious intravenous albumin infusion to improve oncotic pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Midodrine to increase systemic vascular resistance and improve blood pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Low-dose hydralazine to reduce afterload and improve forward cardiac output

explanation.summary: Digoxin is specifically contraindicated in cardiac amyloidosis. Amyloid fibrils bind digoxin, increasing its effective concentration in the myocardium and predisposing to potentially fatal toxicity even at therapeutic serum levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cardiac amyloidosis, amyloid fibrils within the myocardium have a high affinity for binding digoxin. This leads to enhanced local drug concentration and markedly increased sensitivity to digoxin's toxic effects, including life-threatening arrhythmias, even at standard therapeutic doses. Digoxin is therefore specifically contraindicated regardless of the indication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metolazone can be used cautiously in amyloid cardiomyopathy to augment loop diuretic efficacy when volume overload is refractory. While it requires careful monitoring for hypovolemia and electrolyte abnormalities, it is not specifically contraindicated the way digoxin is.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intravenous albumin may be used in patients with amyloidosis who have nephrotic syndrome and severe hypoalbuminemia to help mobilize fluid. It does not carry the specific risk of toxicity seen with digoxin binding to amyloid fibrils.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Midodrine, an alpha-1 agonist, can be used to treat symptomatic orthostatic hypotension in amyloidosis, particularly when autonomic neuropathy is present. It does not interact with amyloid fibrils and is not specifically contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While vasodilators must be used cautiously in cardiac amyloidosis due to the fixed stroke volume and risk of hypotension, hydralazine does not carry the specific risk of tissue-level drug accumulation and toxicity that makes digoxin uniquely dangerous in this condition.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man from tropical West Africa presents with ascites, hepatomegaly, and severe lower extremity edema. He denies chest pain or palpitations. Echocardiography reveals obliteration of the right ventricular apex by dense fibrotic tissue, a small right ventricular cavity, preserved left ventricular function, and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. There is no pericardial effusion or thickening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy with fibrofatty replacement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ebstein anomaly with apical displacement of the tricuspid valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right ventricular infarction with aneurysm formation at the apex

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiac tamponade from tuberculous pericardial effusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endomyocardial fibrosis with apical obliteration of the right ventricle

explanation.summary: This young man from tropical West Africa presents with predominantly right-sided heart failure, apical obliteration of the right ventricle by fibrotic tissue, and tricuspid regurgitation. This is the classic presentation of endomyocardial fibrosis (EMF), the most common form of restrictive cardiomyopathy in tropical regions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endomyocardial fibrosis is endemic in tropical Africa, particularly West Africa, and affects young adults. It is characterized by dense fibrosis of the endocardium and inner myocardium, predominantly affecting the ventricular apices and causing cavity obliteration. When the right ventricle is primarily involved, it presents with right-sided heart failure (ascites, hepatomegaly, edema) and tricuspid regurgitation from papillary muscle involvement. Preserved left ventricular function distinguishes RV-dominant EMF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ARVC involves fibrofatty replacement of the right ventricular free wall and presents primarily with ventricular arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death rather than apical obliteration and right-sided heart failure. The pattern of disease involvement differs from the apical fibrotic obliteration seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ebstein anomaly is a congenital defect with apical displacement of the septal and posterior tricuspid leaflets, creating an atrialized portion of the right ventricle. It does not cause apical obliteration by fibrotic tissue and would typically be diagnosed earlier in life.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Right ventricular infarction occurs in the setting of acute coronary syndrome, typically involving the right coronary artery. This patient has no history of chest pain or coronary risk factors, and the apical fibrotic obliteration pattern is inconsistent with a coronary distribution.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac tamponade presents with Beck's triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds) and pericardial effusion. This patient has no pericardial effusion or thickening, and the echocardiographic findings point to an intrinsic myocardial rather than pericardial process.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with progressive dyspnea and is found to have restrictive cardiomyopathy. She asks about the underlying pathophysiology of her condition. Which of the following best describes the fundamental hemodynamic abnormality in restrictive cardiomyopathy regardless of the underlying etiology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired systolic contraction leading to reduced ejection fraction and ventricular dilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction due to systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased pericardial pressure externally compressing the ventricles during diastole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased ventricular compliance resulting in impaired diastolic filling at normal pressures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Severe mitral regurgitation causing volume overload and secondary ventricular failure

explanation.summary: Restrictive cardiomyopathy is fundamentally a disorder of diastolic filling. Regardless of the specific cause (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis, fibrosis), the core hemodynamic problem is decreased ventricular compliance, meaning the stiff ventricles require abnormally high filling pressures to achieve adequate diastolic volume.
explanation.whyCorrect: In restrictive cardiomyopathy, infiltration, fibrosis, or other pathologic processes stiffen the ventricular walls, reducing compliance. The ventricles cannot relax and expand normally during diastole, so filling is impaired unless filling pressures rise substantially. This causes elevated atrial pressures, pulmonary and systemic venous congestion, and heart failure symptoms despite often-preserved systolic function.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Impaired systolic contraction with reduced EF and ventricular dilation describes dilated cardiomyopathy. Restrictive cardiomyopathy typically preserves systolic function and maintains normal or reduced cavity size; the primary defect is diastolic, not systolic.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dynamic LVOT obstruction with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve is the hallmark of obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Restrictive cardiomyopathy does not involve outflow tract obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased pericardial pressure compressing the ventricles during diastole describes constrictive pericarditis or cardiac tamponade. In restrictive cardiomyopathy, the filling impairment originates from the myocardium itself, not from external pericardial constraint.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Severe mitral regurgitation causes volume overload and is a valvular etiology of heart failure. It is not the fundamental pathophysiology of restrictive cardiomyopathy, which involves intrinsic myocardial stiffness and impaired filling.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents with bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome that was treated surgically 3 years ago, progressive heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and peripheral neuropathy. Echocardiography shows biventricular wall thickening, biatrial enlargement, and grade III diastolic dysfunction. Technetium-99m pyrophosphate (Tc-99m PYP) scintigraphy shows intense diffuse myocardial uptake with a heart-to-contralateral lung ratio of 1.8. Serum and urine immunofixation are negative for a monoclonal protein. Which of the following is the most likely type of amyloidosis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: AL amyloidosis from immunoglobulin light chain deposition

choices.label: B
choices.text: AA amyloidosis from serum amyloid A protein deposition

choices.label: C
choices.text: ATTR wild-type (senile) amyloidosis from transthyretin deposition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hereditary ATTR amyloidosis from a transthyretin gene mutation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Beta-2 microglobulin amyloidosis from long-term hemodialysis

explanation.summary: This elderly man with carpal tunnel syndrome, HFpEF, peripheral neuropathy, and positive Tc-99m PYP scintigraphy without a monoclonal protein has wild-type transthyretin (ATTR-wt) amyloidosis, formerly known as senile cardiac amyloidosis. Tc-99m PYP has high sensitivity and specificity for ATTR amyloidosis, and the negative immunofixation rules out AL amyloidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: ATTR wild-type amyloidosis results from age-related misfolding and aggregation of normal (non-mutant) transthyretin protein. It predominantly affects men over 65-70 years old and has a strong association with bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome (often preceding cardiac diagnosis by years), HFpEF, and peripheral neuropathy. Tc-99m PYP scintigraphy avidly detects ATTR amyloid in the myocardium. The negative immunofixation excludes AL amyloidosis, making this a noninvasive diagnostic pathway for ATTR-wt.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AL amyloidosis requires a clonal plasma cell disorder producing monoclonal light chains. The negative serum and urine immunofixation makes AL amyloidosis very unlikely. Additionally, Tc-99m PYP scintigraphy is much more specific for ATTR than AL amyloidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AA amyloidosis occurs in chronic inflammatory conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, chronic infections) and primarily affects the kidneys and liver. Cardiac involvement in AA amyloidosis is uncommon, and this patient has no chronic inflammatory disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hereditary ATTR amyloidosis is caused by mutations in the transthyretin gene and typically presents at a younger age than wild-type ATTR. While it cannot be definitively excluded without genetic testing, the advanced age of onset, male sex, and absence of family history favor wild-type ATTR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-2 microglobulin amyloidosis occurs in patients on long-term hemodialysis and primarily affects joints and bones (causing carpal tunnel and destructive arthropathy). There is no history of renal failure or dialysis in this patient, and cardiac involvement is rare in this type.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, and peripheral edema. She has a history of Hodgkin lymphoma treated with mediastinal radiation therapy 15 years ago. Echocardiography shows normal left ventricular cavity size, an ejection fraction of 55%, moderate diastolic dysfunction, and mild pericardial thickening. Cardiac catheterization reveals elevated and equalized diastolic pressures. Respiratory variation in ventricular pressures demonstrates concordant changes in left and right ventricular systolic pressures during respiration. Which of the following does the respiratory concordance pattern most support?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constrictive pericarditis as the predominant pathology

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac tamponade from recurrent malignant pericardial effusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Combined constrictive pericarditis and restrictive cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Radiation-induced restrictive cardiomyopathy as the predominant pathology

choices.label: E
choices.text: Severe tricuspid regurgitation from radiation-induced valvular damage

explanation.summary: In this patient with prior mediastinal radiation, the equalized diastolic pressures could suggest either constriction or restriction. The key differentiator is the respiratory variation pattern: concordant changes in RV and LV systolic pressures (both rise with inspiration, both fall with expiration) favor restrictive cardiomyopathy, while discordant changes favor constrictive pericarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In restrictive cardiomyopathy, the pericardium is compliant, so normal respiratory hemodynamic transmission occurs. During inspiration, increased venous return raises both RV and LV filling and systolic pressures concordantly. In constrictive pericarditis, the rigid pericardium creates ventricular interdependence: increased RV filling with inspiration occurs at the expense of LV filling (discordance). Concordance thus supports restriction as the predominant pathology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Constrictive pericarditis produces discordant respiratory variation in ventricular systolic pressures due to enhanced ventricular interdependence within the rigid pericardium. The concordant pattern observed here argues against constriction as the predominant process.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiac tamponade presents acutely with pulsus paradoxus, equalization of diastolic pressures, and hemodynamic collapse. This patient has a chronic presentation, and the absence of a significant pericardial effusion makes tamponade unlikely.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While radiation can cause both pericardial and myocardial disease simultaneously, the concordant respiratory variation specifically indicates that the restrictive component predominates. If constriction were equally contributing, discordant variation would be expected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Severe tricuspid regurgitation would cause prominent v waves in the right atrial tracing and RV volume overload but does not produce the hemodynamic pattern of equalized diastolic pressures or concordant respiratory variation described here.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man from Egypt presents with progressive dyspnea and abdominal distension. He has a history of marked peripheral eosinophilia (absolute eosinophil count 8,000/μL) for the past 2 years. Echocardiography shows thickening and fibrosis of the posterior mitral valve leaflet, obliteration of the left ventricular apex with echogenic material, and an apical thrombus. Which phase of eosinophilic endomyocardial disease is this patient most likely in?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute necrotic phase with eosinophilic infiltration and myocyte damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thrombotic-necrotic phase with mural thrombus overlying damaged endocardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrotic phase with endocardial fibrosis and apical cavity obliteration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyperacute phase with direct eosinophil degranulation causing widespread necrosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Resolution phase with organized scarring and recovered ventricular function

explanation.summary: Loeffler endocarditis (eosinophilic endomyocardial disease) progresses through three stages: (1) acute necrotic phase with eosinophilic infiltration, (2) thrombotic phase with mural thrombus formation, and (3) fibrotic phase with endomyocardial fibrosis and apical obliteration. This patient's echocardiographic findings of apical obliteration, endocardial fibrosis, and valvular involvement indicate the late fibrotic phase.
explanation.whyCorrect: The fibrotic phase of Loeffler endocarditis represents the end-stage of eosinophilic cardiac damage. After the initial necrotic and thrombotic phases, the endocardium undergoes progressive fibrosis leading to restrictive physiology with apical cavity obliteration. The thickened fibrotic mitral valve leaflet and organized apical material are hallmarks of this phase. Despite the persistent eosinophilia, the structural changes indicate the disease has progressed to fibrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The acute necrotic phase occurs within the first few weeks and is characterized by eosinophilic myocarditis with myocyte necrosis. Patients may present with fever and acute heart failure, but echocardiography would not yet show the fibrotic obliteration and valvular thickening seen in this chronic presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The thrombotic phase follows the necrotic phase and features mural thrombus formation over the damaged endocardium. While this patient does have a thrombus, the predominant findings of endocardial fibrosis, apical obliteration, and valvular involvement indicate progression beyond the thrombotic stage into the fibrotic phase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no recognized hyperacute phase in the classic three-stage classification of Loeffler endocarditis. The earliest phase is the acute necrotic phase involving eosinophilic infiltration and degranulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no recognized resolution phase with recovered ventricular function in Loeffler endocarditis. The fibrotic changes are irreversible and lead to permanent restrictive physiology. Ventricular function does not recover once fibrotic apical obliteration has occurred.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with recently diagnosed cardiac AL amyloidosis and an ejection fraction of 45% is being evaluated for treatment. His serum NT-proBNP is 8,500 pg/mL and cardiac troponin T is 0.08 ng/mL. Bone marrow biopsy shows 12% clonal plasma cells. Which of the following therapies targets the underlying cause of this patient's cardiac disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Orthotopic heart transplantation to replace the amyloid-infiltrated myocardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tafamidis to stabilize transthyretin tetramers and prevent fibril formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement for primary prevention of sudden death

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aggressive diuretic therapy to reduce myocardial wall stress and prevent further deposition

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bortezomib-based chemotherapy to eliminate the clonal plasma cell population

explanation.summary: AL amyloidosis results from a clonal plasma cell dyscrasia producing misfolded immunoglobulin light chains that deposit as amyloid fibrils in organs. The definitive treatment targets the underlying plasma cell clone using chemotherapy regimens such as bortezomib (a proteasome inhibitor) combined with cyclophosphamide and dexamethasone (CyBorD).
explanation.whyCorrect: Bortezomib-based chemotherapy (typically CyBorD: cyclophosphamide, bortezomib, dexamethasone) directly targets the clonal plasma cells responsible for producing the pathogenic light chains. By eliminating the source of amyloidogenic protein, hematologic response can be achieved, halting further amyloid deposition. Organ improvement, including cardiac function, may follow if the disease is caught before irreversible fibrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heart transplantation may be considered in selected patients with AL amyloidosis, but it does not address the underlying plasma cell clone. Without concurrent chemotherapy, amyloid will rapidly redeposit in the transplanted heart. Transplant alone does not target the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tafamidis stabilizes the transthyretin tetramer and is specifically indicated for ATTR amyloidosis (both wild-type and hereditary). It has no role in AL amyloidosis, where the amyloidogenic protein is an immunoglobulin light chain, not transthyretin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ICD placement addresses arrhythmic sudden death risk but does not target the underlying amyloid deposition. Furthermore, the benefit of ICDs in cardiac amyloidosis is debated, as the mechanism of death is often pulseless electrical activity rather than shockable ventricular arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diuretics manage volume overload symptoms but do not reduce amyloid deposition or address the underlying clonal plasma cell disease. They are purely supportive and cannot alter the natural history of AL amyloidosis.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male collegiate soccer player collapses on the field during practice and is found to be in pulseless ventricular tachycardia. He is successfully resuscitated with defibrillation. He has a family history of sudden cardiac death in a paternal uncle at age 30. Cardiac MRI reveals right ventricular dilation, regional dyskinesia of the right ventricular free wall, and fibrofatty replacement of the RV myocardium. Genetic testing reveals a pathogenic mutation in the plakophilin-2 (PKP2) gene. Which of the following best describes the molecular mechanism underlying this patient's disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired desmosomal cell-cell adhesion leading to cardiomyocyte detachment and fibrofatty replacement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defective sarcomeric proteins causing myocyte disarray and interstitial fibrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dysfunctional ion channel proteins causing abnormal cardiac repolarization and arrhythmias

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abnormal extracellular matrix deposition from defective collagen cross-linking enzymes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitochondrial dysfunction causing impaired oxidative phosphorylation and myocyte death

explanation.summary: This patient with exercise-triggered ventricular tachycardia, family history of sudden death, RV fibrofatty replacement on MRI, and a PKP2 mutation has arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC). ARVC is caused by mutations in desmosomal genes, with PKP2 being the most commonly affected gene.
explanation.whyCorrect: Desmosomes are intercellular junctions that mechanically couple adjacent cardiomyocytes. PKP2 encodes plakophilin-2, a critical desmosomal protein. Mutations impair desmosomal integrity, causing cardiomyocytes to detach under mechanical stress (especially during exercise). Detached myocytes undergo apoptosis and are progressively replaced by fibrofatty tissue, creating the substrate for re-entrant ventricular arrhythmias. This explains the predilection for the RV free wall, which experiences greater mechanical stress.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Defective sarcomeric proteins (beta-myosin heavy chain, myosin-binding protein C) cause hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, which presents with asymmetric septal hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, and myocyte disarray rather than RV fibrofatty replacement.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ion channel mutations cause channelopathies such as long QT syndrome, Brugada syndrome, and catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia. These conditions produce arrhythmias without structural myocardial disease or fibrofatty replacement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Defective extracellular matrix proteins cause conditions like Marfan syndrome or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, affecting connective tissues and large vessels. They do not cause the desmosomal disruption and fibrofatty myocardial replacement characteristic of ARVC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitochondrial cardiomyopathies present with hypertrophic or dilated phenotypes, often with multisystem disease (skeletal myopathy, encephalopathy). They do not cause the specific pattern of RV fibrofatty replacement seen in desmosomal disorders.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of palpitations and lightheadedness during exercise. A 12-lead ECG shows T-wave inversions in leads V1 through V4 and an epsilon wave in lead V1. Signal-averaged ECG demonstrates late potentials. Echocardiography shows right ventricular dilation with akinesia of the RV inferior wall. Which of the following ECG findings is most specific for the diagnosis of ARVC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: T-wave inversions in the right precordial leads V1 through V3

choices.label: B
choices.text: Epsilon waves representing delayed right ventricular activation potentials

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prolonged S-wave upstroke duration of 55 ms or greater in leads V1-V3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Premature ventricular complexes with a left bundle branch block morphology

choices.label: E
choices.text: Late potentials detected on signal-averaged electrocardiography

explanation.summary: While several ECG abnormalities occur in ARVC, the epsilon wave is the most specific finding. It represents small-amplitude potentials occurring after the end of the QRS complex, reflecting delayed depolarization through islands of surviving myocardium within fibrofatty scar tissue in the right ventricle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Epsilon waves are low-amplitude signals occurring between the end of the QRS complex and the beginning of the T wave, best seen in leads V1-V3. They result from slow fragmented conduction through the fibrofatty replacement tissue characteristic of ARVC. While present in only about 30% of ARVC patients, epsilon waves are highly specific for the diagnosis and are classified as a major diagnostic criterion in the 2010 Task Force Criteria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right precordial T-wave inversions in V1-V3 are a major diagnostic criterion for ARVC in patients over age 14 (since they can be a normal variant in younger individuals). However, they are less specific than epsilon waves because they also occur in right ventricular hypertrophy, pulmonary embolism, and Brugada syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prolonged S-wave upstroke (terminal activation duration ≥55 ms) in V1-V3 is a minor diagnostic criterion for ARVC representing delayed RV activation. It is more sensitive but less specific than epsilon waves for the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PVCs with LBBB morphology indicate a right ventricular origin and are seen in ARVC but are not specific. LBBB-morphology PVCs also occur with right ventricular outflow tract tachycardia, a benign idiopathic condition, and other causes of RV ectopy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Late potentials on signal-averaged ECG are a minor diagnostic criterion for ARVC. They indicate zones of slow conduction but are also seen in other conditions causing myocardial scarring, such as post-myocardial infarction, making them less specific than epsilon waves.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man is diagnosed with ARVC after genetic testing reveals a desmoglein-2 (DSG2) mutation following the sudden cardiac death of his older brother. He is currently asymptomatic with a normal resting ECG and no ventricular arrhythmias on 24-hour Holter monitoring. Cardiac MRI shows subtle right ventricular free wall fatty infiltration without regional wall motion abnormalities. His family asks about exercise restrictions. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation regarding physical activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No exercise restrictions are necessary since the patient is asymptomatic and structurally normal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Restriction from competitive and high-intensity endurance sports with allowance of low-intensity recreational activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complete restriction from all physical activity including walking and light household tasks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restriction only from contact sports to prevent blunt cardiac trauma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Exercise is permitted after prophylactic implantation of a cardioverter-defibrillator

explanation.summary: In ARVC, intense exercise accelerates disease progression and increases arrhythmic risk. Current expert consensus recommends that all patients with ARVC, including asymptomatic gene carriers with early disease, restrict from competitive and high-intensity endurance sports while allowing low-intensity recreational activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Exercise, particularly endurance and high-intensity sports, is a powerful trigger for ventricular arrhythmias in ARVC and accelerates the pathological process of fibrofatty replacement. Mechanical stress on desmosome-deficient cardiomyocytes promotes cell detachment and death. Multiple studies have shown that ARVC patients who continue high-intensity exercise develop symptoms earlier, have more arrhythmias, and have faster disease progression. Low-intensity recreational activity is considered safe and important for overall health.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Even asymptomatic ARVC gene carriers should be counseled to avoid competitive and high-intensity exercise. The desmosomal defect makes the RV myocardium vulnerable to mechanical stress-induced damage, and vigorous exercise can precipitate the first arrhythmic event. Absence of current symptoms does not eliminate risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Complete restriction from all physical activity is excessively restrictive and unnecessary. Low-intensity activities such as walking, light cycling, and recreational swimming are not associated with increased arrhythmic risk and are important for psychological well-being and cardiovascular health.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The exercise restriction in ARVC is not about blunt cardiac trauma. The risk is from the hemodynamic stress of exercise itself, which increases RV wall stress, accelerates fibrofatty replacement, and triggers re-entrant arrhythmias through the diseased substrate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An ICD does not make competitive exercise safe in ARVC. While ICDs save lives by terminating ventricular arrhythmias, they do not prevent the progression of fibrofatty replacement driven by exercise-induced mechanical stress. Furthermore, ICD shocks during exercise carry additional risks including inappropriate shocks and lead fracture.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known ARVC presents with sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia with a left bundle branch block morphology and superior axis. He is hemodynamically stable. After successful cardioversion, his cardiologist explains that the arrhythmia originated from a specific region of the right ventricle. Which of the following anatomic regions of the right ventricle is classically affected earliest and most commonly in ARVC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The right ventricular outflow tract, interventricular septum, and right ventricular apex forming the triangle of dysplasia

choices.label: B
choices.text: The right ventricular inflow tract, right ventricular outflow tract, and right ventricular apex forming the triangle of dysplasia

choices.label: C
choices.text: The interventricular septum extending from base to apex bilaterally

choices.label: D
choices.text: The right ventricular free wall uniformly from base to apex

choices.label: E
choices.text: The right ventricular papillary muscles and chordae tendineae

explanation.summary: ARVC classically affects three regions of the right ventricle that form the so-called 'triangle of dysplasia': the right ventricular inflow tract (subtricuspid region), the right ventricular outflow tract (infundibulum), and the right ventricular apex. These areas are most susceptible to the fibrofatty replacement process.
explanation.whyCorrect: The triangle of dysplasia was first described by Marcus et al. and refers to the three RV regions most vulnerable to the desmosomal dysfunction of ARVC: (1) the subtricuspid inflow region, (2) the RVOT/infundibulum, and (3) the RV apex. These areas experience the greatest mechanical wall stress in the thin-walled right ventricle, making desmosome-deficient myocytes most susceptible to detachment and fibrofatty replacement at these sites. The VT with LBBB morphology and superior axis in this patient suggests origin from the inferior RV wall/inflow region.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This answer incorrectly includes the interventricular septum as part of the triangle of dysplasia. The septum is typically spared in classic ARVC because it is structurally supported by both ventricles and experiences less wall stress. The correct third component is the RV inflow tract, not the septum.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The interventricular septum is characteristically spared in ARVC, particularly in early disease. Septal involvement may occur in advanced disease or in biventricular variants but is not the classic early pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ARVC does not affect the RV free wall uniformly. It shows a predilection for the three regions forming the triangle of dysplasia, reflecting the regional variation in mechanical wall stress across the right ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The papillary muscles and chordae are not the primary targets in ARVC. Tricuspid regurgitation in ARVC results from RV dilation and annular dilatation rather than primary papillary muscle or chordal involvement.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with ARVC and a pathogenic desmoplakin (DSP) mutation is being followed with serial imaging. Unlike classic ARVC, her cardiac MRI shows predominantly left ventricular involvement with subepicardial and mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement in the inferolateral wall and relative sparing of the right ventricle. Which of the following best describes this phenotypic variant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy caused by a coincidental titin mutation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myocarditis mimicking ARVC due to viral desmosomal tropism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with secondary right ventricular involvement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy with left-dominant disease expression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ischemic cardiomyopathy from premature coronary artery disease

explanation.summary: ARVC exists on a spectrum now increasingly recognized as arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM). Certain desmosomal mutations, particularly in desmoplakin (DSP), are associated with a left-dominant phenotype where the left ventricle is primarily affected with subepicardial/mid-wall fibrosis while the right ventricle is relatively spared.
explanation.whyCorrect: The traditional concept of ARVC as an exclusively right ventricular disease has evolved. Desmoplakin mutations, in particular, are associated with left-dominant arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy. The LV involvement typically shows a characteristic subepicardial and mid-wall pattern of fibrosis (seen as late gadolinium enhancement on MRI) in the inferolateral wall. This phenotype carries a risk of ventricular arrhythmias and sudden death comparable to classic RV-dominant ARVC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The patient has a confirmed pathogenic DSP mutation, which is a well-established desmosomal gene causing arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy. Attributing the phenotype to a coincidental titin mutation ignores the known genotype-phenotype correlation of DSP mutations with LV-dominant disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While myocarditis can mimic ARVC on imaging, this patient has a confirmed pathogenic desmosomal mutation explaining the phenotype. The subepicardial LGE pattern is characteristic of desmoplakin-related cardiomyopathy, not a viral infection of desmosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is caused by sarcomeric protein mutations and presents with ventricular hypertrophy, not the subepicardial fibrosis pattern described. HCM does not arise from desmosomal gene mutations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ischemic cardiomyopathy causes subendocardial or transmural LGE in coronary artery territories, not the subepicardial/mid-wall pattern seen in this patient. A 25-year-old with a desmosomal mutation and this LGE pattern does not have premature coronary disease.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with ARVC who has survived a prior episode of sustained ventricular tachycardia undergoes implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) placement for secondary prevention. Six months later, he presents with 12 appropriate ICD shocks in the past month despite therapy with sotalol. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management to reduce his arrhythmia burden?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the sotalol dose to maximum tolerated level and continue monitoring

choices.label: B
choices.text: Switch from sotalol to flecainide for more effective sodium channel blockade

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add amiodarone or perform catheter ablation of the ventricular tachycardia circuit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deactivate the ICD and initiate oral mexiletine for arrhythmia suppression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Proceed directly to orthotopic heart transplantation for refractory arrhythmias

explanation.summary: This ARVC patient is experiencing frequent appropriate ICD shocks (electrical storm) despite sotalol therapy, indicating refractory ventricular arrhythmias. The next step is either adding amiodarone (the most effective antiarrhythmic for VT suppression) or catheter ablation targeting the re-entrant circuits within the fibrofatty scar tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: For ARVC patients with frequent ICD shocks despite beta-blocker or sotalol therapy, the recommended approach is escalation to amiodarone (most effective antiarrhythmic for VT suppression in ARVC) and/or catheter ablation. Ablation in ARVC typically uses an epicardial approach because the fibrofatty substrate often involves the epicardial and mid-myocardial layers. Combined endocardial-epicardial ablation can significantly reduce VT burden and ICD shocks.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Simply increasing sotalol without additional intervention is insufficient for a patient experiencing 12 shocks per month. This frequency constitutes electrical storm, which requires escalation of therapy rather than continued observation with dose titration alone.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Flecainide (a Class IC antiarrhythmic) is contraindicated in structural heart disease including ARVC because it can paradoxically increase arrhythmia risk and mortality. The CAST trial demonstrated increased death rates with flecainide in patients with structural heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Deactivating an ICD in a patient with recurrent sustained VT and prior cardiac arrest would be dangerous and inappropriate. The ICD provides life-saving therapy for hemodynamically significant arrhythmias. Mexiletine alone cannot reliably prevent lethal arrhythmias in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heart transplantation is reserved for end-stage ARVC with refractory heart failure or truly intractable arrhythmias despite maximal medical therapy and catheter ablation. It is not appropriate as the immediate next step before attempting amiodarone or ablation.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy is referred for cardiac evaluation after his father was diagnosed with ARVC. The boy is asymptomatic and plays on the school basketball team. Genetic testing reveals the same pathogenic PKP2 mutation as his father. His resting ECG is normal, echocardiography shows a structurally normal heart, and 24-hour Holter monitoring reveals no arrhythmias. Which of the following best describes this patient's status and the concept of incomplete penetrance in ARVC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He does not carry the disease gene and his positive genetic test represents a laboratory error

choices.label: B
choices.text: He has the genetic substrate for ARVC but may never develop clinical manifestations due to incomplete penetrance

choices.label: C
choices.text: He will inevitably develop full clinical ARVC by age 30 because desmosomal mutations are fully penetrant

choices.label: D
choices.text: His normal evaluation confirms that ARVC skips generations and his children are not at risk

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pathogenic mutation must be homozygous to cause disease, and he is a heterozygous carrier

explanation.summary: ARVC demonstrates autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance and variable expressivity. A pathogenic mutation carrier may have a completely normal cardiac evaluation at one time point yet develop disease manifestations later, or may never develop clinical disease. This patient is a genotype-positive, phenotype-negative individual who requires ongoing surveillance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Incomplete penetrance means that not all individuals carrying a pathogenic mutation will express the clinical phenotype. In ARVC, penetrance is estimated at 30-50% depending on the gene and mutation. This patient carries the PKP2 mutation but currently shows no clinical manifestations. He may develop disease over time (age-related penetrance) or may remain phenotype-negative. Environmental modifiers, particularly exercise intensity, also influence disease expression. Serial clinical surveillance every 1-3 years is essential.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The genetic test identified the same pathogenic mutation as his affected father, making laboratory error extremely unlikely. Confirmed pathogenic variants in desmosomal genes are clinically meaningful even in the absence of current phenotypic expression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ARVC desmosomal mutations are not fully penetrant. Incomplete penetrance is a hallmark of the disease, meaning a significant proportion of mutation carriers never develop clinical disease. Predicting inevitable disease development by a specific age is not possible.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ARVC does not skip generations. It follows autosomal dominant inheritance, meaning each child of a mutation carrier has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation. A genotype-positive individual can transmit the mutation regardless of their own phenotypic expression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ARVC is predominantly an autosomal dominant disorder, meaning a single pathogenic allele (heterozygous state) is sufficient to cause disease. While compound or digenic heterozygosity can produce more severe phenotypes, homozygosity is not required for disease expression.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man with ARVC and recurrent sustained ventricular tachycardia undergoes electrophysiology study. Electroanatomic voltage mapping of the right ventricle reveals extensive areas of low voltage (<0.5 mV) along the RV free wall extending from the subtricuspid region to the outflow tract. During programmed stimulation, three distinct VT morphologies are inducible. Which of the following best explains why multiple VT morphologies are inducible in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiple independent reentrant circuits exist within the heterogeneous fibrofatty scar tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Triggered activity from delayed afterdepolarizations occurs at multiple foci simultaneously

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhanced automaticity in multiple ectopic pacemaker cells throughout the right ventricle

choices.label: D
choices.text: A single fixed reentrant circuit with varying conduction velocities producing different QRS morphologies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Early afterdepolarizations triggered by QT prolongation from antiarrhythmic medication

explanation.summary: In ARVC, the fibrofatty replacement of myocardium creates a heterogeneous substrate with islands of surviving cardiomyocytes interspersed within scar tissue. This creates multiple potential reentrant circuits with different entry and exit points, explaining why multiple VT morphologies can be induced during electrophysiology study.
explanation.whyCorrect: The extensive low-voltage areas on electroanatomic mapping represent regions of fibrofatty scar. Within these scars, surviving myocyte bundles form conducting channels (isthmuses) that can support reentrant circuits. Because the scar is heterogeneous and extensive, multiple distinct conducting channels exist, each capable of supporting a separate reentrant VT circuit with its own exit point and therefore its own QRS morphology. This is why catheter ablation in ARVC often requires targeting multiple circuits.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Triggered activity from delayed afterdepolarizations is not the primary mechanism of VT in ARVC. The extensive low-voltage scar and inducibility with programmed stimulation are hallmarks of scar-related reentry, not triggered activity, which is more associated with digitalis toxicity or catecholaminergic states.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Enhanced automaticity produces focal VT that is typically not inducible by programmed stimulation. The fact that the VTs were inducible by programmed electrical stimulation strongly supports a reentrant mechanism rather than abnormal automaticity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A single fixed reentrant circuit would produce a single VT morphology. Multiple inducible morphologies require multiple distinct circuits with different exit points, producing different QRS vector patterns on the surface ECG.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Early afterdepolarizations are associated with acquired or congenital long QT syndrome and typically produce polymorphic VT (torsades de pointes), not monomorphic VT. The three distinct monomorphic VT morphologies in this patient reflect multiple scar-related reentrant circuits.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with biopsy-confirmed ARVC presents with progressive exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Echocardiography shows severe RV dilation with an RV fractional area change of 15% (normal >35%), moderate RV systolic dysfunction, severe tricuspid regurgitation, and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35%. His right atrial pressure is estimated at 20 mmHg. Despite optimal medical therapy with diuretics, ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and aldosterone antagonist, his symptoms have not improved after 6 months. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add digoxin to improve biventricular contractility and slow atrioventricular conduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform tricuspid valve replacement to reduce right ventricular volume overload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiate cardiac resynchronization therapy with biventricular pacing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer for evaluation for orthotopic heart transplantation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Implant a right ventricular assist device as destination therapy

explanation.summary: This patient has advanced biventricular heart failure from ARVC refractory to optimal medical therapy. With severe RV dysfunction, severe TR, LV involvement (EF 35%), and persistent NYHA class III-IV symptoms despite 6 months of guideline-directed medical therapy, referral for heart transplant evaluation is the most appropriate next step.
explanation.whyCorrect: Orthotopic heart transplantation is the definitive treatment for end-stage ARVC with refractory heart failure. This patient demonstrates progressive biventricular failure despite optimal medical therapy, meeting criteria for transplant evaluation. ARVC patients who progress to biventricular failure have a poor prognosis without transplant. Transplant outcomes for ARVC are generally favorable, comparable to other cardiomyopathies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Digoxin provides modest symptomatic benefit in heart failure but does not improve mortality. In this patient with advanced biventricular failure refractory to comprehensive medical therapy, adding digoxin is unlikely to produce meaningful clinical improvement and does not address the need for definitive therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tricuspid valve replacement in ARVC is generally not recommended because the tricuspid regurgitation is secondary to progressive RV dilation from the underlying myopathic process. Surgical correction of the valve does not address the diseased RV myocardium and carries high surgical risk in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac resynchronization therapy is indicated for heart failure with reduced EF and evidence of electrical dyssynchrony (typically LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms). CRT has not been shown to benefit ARVC-related heart failure and does not address the underlying fibrofatty myocardial degeneration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Right ventricular assist devices are not standard therapy for ARVC. The thin, fibrofatty RV wall makes surgical cannulation technically challenging and prone to complications. Additionally, this patient has biventricular dysfunction, which a single-ventricle assist device would not adequately address.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a heart specimen from a 24-year-old man who died suddenly during a marathon. Gross examination reveals a dilated right ventricle with translucent areas on the RV free wall. Histologic examination shows extensive replacement of right ventricular myocardium with adipose tissue and fibrosis, with residual islands of cardiomyocytes surrounded by fibrous bands. Which of the following additional histologic findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of ARVC over isolated cardiac lipomatosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The presence of adipose tissue constituting more than 3% of the right ventricular myocardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Residual cardiomyocyte atrophy and degeneration with accompanying inflammatory infiltrates

choices.label: C
choices.text: A sharp border between adipose tissue and adjacent normal myocardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Predominant fatty infiltration limited to the epicardial surface without transmural extension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uniform distribution of adipocytes without associated fibrosis or myocyte loss

explanation.summary: Distinguishing ARVC from benign cardiac lipomatosis (fatty infiltration of the heart) requires careful histologic analysis. The key differentiating features of ARVC include cardiomyocyte degeneration, replacement-type fibrosis accompanying the fat, and inflammatory infiltrates, reflecting active myocyte death rather than simple fat accumulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In ARVC, the fibrofatty replacement is a consequence of ongoing cardiomyocyte death from desmosomal dysfunction. Histologically, this manifests as myocyte atrophy, degenerating myocytes, replacement fibrosis intermingled with adipocytes, and patchy inflammatory infiltrates (often lymphocytic). These findings indicate an active myopathic process. In contrast, simple cardiac lipomatosis shows mature adipocytes without myocyte degeneration, fibrosis, or inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A quantitative threshold of adipose tissue percentage alone cannot distinguish ARVC from cardiac lipomatosis. Both conditions can show substantial fatty infiltration. The qualitative features of myocyte degeneration and fibrosis are far more important diagnostically than the absolute percentage of fat.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A sharp border between fat and normal myocardium is more characteristic of lipomatous metaplasia or lipoma rather than ARVC. In ARVC, the transition between fibrofatty tissue and surviving myocardium is typically irregular with interdigitating bands of fibrosis and surviving myocyte islands.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epicardial fatty infiltration without transmural extension is a normal finding, particularly in obese and elderly individuals. ARVC characteristically shows transmural or near-transmural fibrofatty replacement extending from the epicardium through to the endocardium, which correlates with the translucent areas seen grossly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uniform adipocyte distribution without fibrosis or myocyte loss describes benign cardiac lipomatosis, which is the very condition the pathologist is trying to exclude. The absence of fibrosis and myocyte degeneration would argue against ARVC.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of poorly controlled hypertension presents with progressive exertional dyspnea over the past 6 months. Echocardiography reveals concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, a left ventricular ejection fraction of 65%, an E/A ratio of 0.7, and an elevated E/e' ratio of 16. Left ventricular end-diastolic pressure is estimated at 22 mmHg. Which of the following best explains the primary mechanism of this patient's heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired ventricular relaxation and increased passive chamber stiffness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased myocardial contractility due to sarcomere loss

choices.label: C
choices.text: Volume overload from excessive neurohormonal activation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced preload due to decreased venous return

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased afterload from fixed valvular obstruction

explanation.summary: This patient has heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) secondary to hypertensive heart disease. The concentric LVH, preserved EF, low E/A ratio, and elevated E/e' ratio indicate diastolic dysfunction characterized by impaired relaxation and increased chamber stiffness.
explanation.whyCorrect: In HFpEF, chronic pressure overload leads to concentric LVH with increased interstitial fibrosis and cardiomyocyte hypertrophy. This causes both impaired active relaxation (reduced lusitropy) and increased passive stiffness of the ventricle, shifting the end-diastolic pressure-volume relationship upward and leftward. The elevated E/e' ratio reflects elevated filling pressures despite a normal EF.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased contractility with sarcomere loss is characteristic of systolic heart failure (HFrEF), such as dilated cardiomyopathy. This patient's EF is preserved at 65%, indicating intact systolic function.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While neurohormonal activation occurs in HFpEF, the primary mechanism is not volume overload. The fundamental problem is impaired diastolic filling due to structural changes in the myocardium, not primary volume excess.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reduced preload would not cause elevated filling pressures. This patient has elevated LVEDP (22 mmHg), indicating the opposite problem: the ventricle requires higher filling pressures to achieve adequate diastolic volume due to reduced compliance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no mention of valvular disease. The patient's heart failure is due to hypertensive myocardial remodeling, not a fixed valvular obstruction such as aortic stenosis.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with ischemic cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 25% is found to have a serum norepinephrine level three times the upper limit of normal. Her plasma renin activity and aldosterone levels are also markedly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely direct consequence of chronic sympathetic nervous system activation in this patient's failing heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Downregulation of beta-1 adrenergic receptors on cardiomyocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased afterload through systemic vasodilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased parasympathetic tone to the sinoatrial node

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suppression of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enhanced diastolic relaxation through increased cAMP

explanation.summary: Chronic sympathetic activation in heart failure leads to sustained catecholamine exposure, causing beta-1 adrenergic receptor downregulation and desensitization. This is a hallmark of the failing heart and contributes to diminished inotropic reserve.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic elevation of norepinephrine causes internalization and degradation of beta-1 receptors, reducing their density on cardiomyocytes by up to 50%. Additionally, increased beta-adrenergic receptor kinase (GRK2/beta-ARK1) phosphorylates remaining receptors, uncoupling them from their Gs-protein signaling cascade. This blunts the heart's response to catecholamines and is the rationale for beta-blocker therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic sympathetic activation causes systemic vasoconstriction (not vasodilation) through alpha-1 receptor stimulation, increasing afterload and further burdening the failing heart.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heart failure is characterized by autonomic imbalance with sympathetic predominance and withdrawal of parasympathetic tone, leading to reduced heart rate variability and elevated resting heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sympathetic activation stimulates (not suppresses) the RAAS through beta-1 receptor-mediated renin release from juxtaglomerular cells, creating a maladaptive neurohormonal positive feedback loop.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While acute beta-1 stimulation increases cAMP and enhances lusitropy, chronic overstimulation causes receptor downregulation and desensitization, reducing cAMP production and impairing both contractility and relaxation.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with newly diagnosed heart failure has an echocardiogram showing an ejection fraction of 20% and severe global hypokinesis. A pressure-volume loop analysis is performed. Compared to a normal heart, which of the following changes would be expected on his pressure-volume diagram?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shift of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship to the left with a steeper slope

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased end-diastolic volume with preserved stroke volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shift of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship to the right with a decreased slope

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased end-systolic volume with unchanged end-diastolic volume

choices.label: E
choices.text: Narrowing of the pressure-volume loop with increased peak systolic pressure

explanation.summary: In systolic heart failure, reduced contractility causes the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR) to shift rightward with a decreased slope (reduced Ees), reflecting diminished ability to eject blood. The PV loop widens to the right with increased end-diastolic and end-systolic volumes.
explanation.whyCorrect: The slope of the ESPVR (Ees, or end-systolic elastance) is a load-independent measure of contractility. In systolic HF, reduced contractility decreases this slope and shifts the relationship rightward, meaning the ventricle cannot generate the same pressure for a given volume. The loop shifts right with increased EDV (Frank-Starling compensation) and markedly increased ESV, resulting in reduced stroke volume and ejection fraction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A leftward shift with a steeper ESPVR slope would indicate increased contractility (positive inotropic state), the opposite of what occurs in systolic heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: In systolic HF, end-diastolic volume increases (not decreases) as the ventricle dilates to compensate via the Frank-Starling mechanism. Stroke volume is reduced, not preserved.
whyWrongByChoice.D: End-systolic volume increases (not decreases) because the weakened ventricle cannot adequately empty. This increased residual volume is a defining feature of systolic dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The PV loop widens to the right and becomes shorter vertically. Peak systolic pressure typically decreases or is maintained only through compensatory vasoconstriction, and the loop does not narrow.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic heart failure and an ejection fraction of 30% is started on a new medication. After 3 months, repeat echocardiography shows a decrease in left ventricular end-diastolic diameter from 6.8 cm to 6.0 cm, improvement in ejection fraction to 40%, and reduced left ventricular mass. Serum norepinephrine levels have also decreased. Which of the following medications most likely produced these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Digoxin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Furosemide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carvedilol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hydralazine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Milrinone

explanation.summary: The findings describe reverse remodeling: decreased LV dimensions, improved EF, reduced LV mass, and decreased norepinephrine. Beta-blockers such as carvedilol are the prototypical agents that promote reverse ventricular remodeling in HFrEF through attenuation of chronic sympathetic overdrive.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carvedilol (a non-selective beta-blocker with alpha-1 blocking activity) counteracts the deleterious effects of chronic sympathetic activation. Over months, it promotes reverse remodeling by reducing myocyte apoptosis, decreasing interstitial fibrosis, improving calcium handling, and allowing recovery of beta-receptor density. The COMET and COPERNICUS trials demonstrated improved LVEF, reduced LV volumes, and mortality benefit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Digoxin is a positive inotrope that mildly improves symptoms and reduces hospitalizations but does not promote reverse remodeling or reduce mortality. It does not significantly reduce LV mass or dimensions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Furosemide reduces volume overload and can decrease LV dimensions acutely through preload reduction, but it does not produce true reverse remodeling, improve EF, or reduce serum norepinephrine levels. It may actually increase neurohormonal activation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydralazine is an arteriolar vasodilator that reduces afterload but does not significantly promote reverse remodeling or decrease sympathetic tone. It is used in combination with isosorbide dinitrate as an alternative to ACE inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Milrinone is a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor used for acute inotropic support. Chronic use increases mortality and does not promote reverse remodeling. It is associated with increased arrhythmias.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying the effects of chronic volume overload on the heart. In an animal model of severe mitral regurgitation sustained over 6 months, she observes increased left ventricular end-diastolic volume, eccentric hypertrophy, and a shift of the diastolic pressure-volume curve to the right. Which of the following best explains the pattern of myocardial remodeling observed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addition of sarcomeres in parallel, increasing wall thickness relative to cavity size

choices.label: B
choices.text: Addition of sarcomeres in series, increasing cavity size relative to wall thickness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Replacement fibrosis without significant change in myocyte length

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased titin expression leading to increased passive stiffness

choices.label: E
choices.text: Myocyte apoptosis with proportional reduction in chamber dimensions

explanation.summary: Chronic volume overload (as in mitral regurgitation) causes eccentric hypertrophy through the addition of sarcomeres in series, elongating individual myocytes. This increases cavity size relative to wall thickness and shifts the diastolic pressure-volume curve rightward, increasing ventricular compliance.
explanation.whyCorrect: In response to volume overload, increased diastolic wall stress triggers sarcomere replication in series (end-to-end), lengthening the myocytes. This produces eccentric hypertrophy with an increased radius-to-wall-thickness ratio. The rightward shift of the diastolic PV curve reflects increased chamber compliance, allowing the ventricle to accommodate larger volumes without proportional pressure increases, consistent with Laplace's law.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Addition of sarcomeres in parallel with increased wall thickness relative to cavity size describes concentric hypertrophy, which is the response to pressure overload (e.g., aortic stenosis, hypertension), not volume overload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Replacement fibrosis without myocyte elongation would increase passive stiffness and shift the diastolic PV curve leftward/upward, not rightward. Fibrosis is more characteristic of ischemic or restrictive cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased titin expression with increased stiffness would shift the diastolic PV curve upward and leftward, characteristic of HFpEF. In volume overload, titin isoform switching actually favors the more compliant N2BA isoform.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Myocyte apoptosis with chamber shrinkage is not the remodeling pattern seen in volume overload. Volume overload causes myocyte hypertrophy and elongation, with chamber dilation rather than reduction.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with peripartum cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 18% is admitted with acute decompensated heart failure. Laboratory studies reveal a serum sodium of 126 mEq/L, BUN of 42 mg/dL, and creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL). Her urine sodium is 8 mEq/L and urine osmolality is 620 mOsm/kg. Which of the following pathophysiological mechanisms best accounts for this patient's hyponatremia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Primary renal sodium wasting from tubular dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excessive free water intake exceeding renal excretory capacity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thiazide diuretic-induced impairment of urinary diluting capacity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adrenal insufficiency from chronic illness causing cortisol deficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: Non-osmotic release of arginine vasopressin due to decreased effective circulating volume

explanation.summary: Hyponatremia in decompensated heart failure is dilutional, caused by non-osmotic (hemodynamic) stimulation of AVP/ADH release. Despite total body sodium and water excess, decreased cardiac output reduces effective arterial blood volume, triggering baroreceptor-mediated AVP release and free water retention.
explanation.whyCorrect: In severe heart failure, reduced cardiac output decreases effective circulating volume despite total body volume expansion. Arterial baroreceptors sense underfilling and stimulate AVP release independent of serum osmolality (non-osmotic release). AVP acts on V2 receptors in the collecting duct, increasing aquaporin-2 insertion and free water reabsorption, producing dilutional hyponatremia. The low urine sodium (8 mEq/L) and concentrated urine (620 mOsm/kg) confirm avid renal sodium and water retention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The urine sodium is very low (8 mEq/L), indicating avid renal sodium retention, not sodium wasting. Primary renal sodium wasting would produce urine sodium >20 mEq/L.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While excessive water intake can contribute, the primary mechanism is impaired free water excretion due to AVP-mediated water retention, not simple polydipsia. The concentrated urine confirms inappropriate water retention despite hypo-osmolality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The patient is not described as taking thiazide diuretics. Thiazides impair diluting capacity in the distal convoluted tubule, but this mechanism is distinct from the neurohormonal-driven hyponatremia of heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adrenal insufficiency can cause hyponatremia but would present with hyperkalemia, hypotension refractory to vasopressors, and eosinophilia. There is no clinical suggestion of adrenal insufficiency in this patient.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with chronic systolic heart failure reports that he can walk one block on flat ground before becoming dyspneic but is comfortable at rest. He sleeps on two pillows but denies paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. His BNP is 480 pg/mL. On the cardiac function curve, his operating point is shifted rightward and downward compared to normal. Which of the following compensatory mechanisms initially helps maintain cardiac output along this curve before eventually contributing to further deterioration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased venous return through sympathetic venoconstriction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary vasoconstriction to reduce left ventricular filling

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myocardial cell death to reduce oxygen demand

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased preload utilization via the Frank-Starling mechanism

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased heart rate to prolong diastolic filling time

explanation.summary: The Frank-Starling mechanism is the primary initial compensatory response in heart failure. Increased preload (end-diastolic volume) stretches sarcomeres toward their optimal length, temporarily increasing stroke volume. However, this mechanism operates on a depressed curve and eventually contributes to pulmonary congestion.
explanation.whyCorrect: In early heart failure, sodium and water retention increases blood volume and preload. On the Frank-Starling curve, increased end-diastolic volume stretches myocardial fibers, increasing the force of contraction and partially maintaining cardiac output. However, the failing heart operates on a flattened curve, so further preload increases yield diminishing returns while progressively elevating filling pressures, causing pulmonary and systemic congestion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sympathetic venoconstriction actually increases (not decreases) venous return by shifting blood from the unstressed venous compartment to the stressed compartment, augmenting preload. This contributes to the Frank-Starling mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary vasoconstriction does not reduce LV filling as a compensatory mechanism. Pulmonary vasoconstriction in heart failure is a pathological consequence of elevated left atrial pressures and contributes to right heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocardial cell death (apoptosis and necrosis) is a maladaptive consequence of neurohormonal activation, not a compensatory mechanism. It worsens cardiac function and contributes to progressive remodeling.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heart rate typically increases (not decreases) in heart failure due to sympathetic activation. Tachycardia is a compensatory mechanism to maintain cardiac output (CO = HR × SV) when stroke volume falls.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with chronic heart failure and an ejection fraction of 22% undergoes right heart catheterization. His pulmonary capillary wedge pressure is 28 mmHg, cardiac index is 1.8 L/min/m², right atrial pressure is 18 mmHg, and pulmonary artery pressure is 58/30 mmHg. The transpulmonary gradient is calculated at 16 mmHg and pulmonary vascular resistance is 4.5 Wood units. Which of the following best characterizes this patient's pulmonary hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isolated pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isolated post-capillary pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension due to chronic thromboembolic disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Combined pre- and post-capillary pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: E
